    "31d7621f-cd63-4b1a-8ce0-9dded478631d": {
      "children": [
        "bd1e5567-1948-4e37-8dbe-56e7186c5a4d"
      ],
      "id": "31d7621f-cd63-4b1a-8ce0-9dded478631d",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": null,
          "role": "user"
        },
        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "The prose for Chapter 5 needs to follow the existing grid and canon, so here is a draft that fits all the files you’ve defined. [file:0a2311af-b53e-4e20-9d0d-c7854ffa99a5][file:3f75f15a-1ea1-4a1a-afd2-6fe88e6bada9][file:9a4d8dfe-4d92-4951-b01e-4468bbe871ed]\r\n\r\n***\r\n\r\n## Chapter 5: Lines That Don’t Lie\r\n\r\nTiffani had to rebuild her map of the street before she could trust what she saw. [file:0a2311af-b53e-4e20-9d0d-c7854ffa99a5][file:3f75f15a-1ea1-4a1a-afd2-6fe88e6bada9]\r\n\r\nThe kiln corridor had shifted in ways her mind kept trying to refuse. Shop fronts she knew at one spacing now sat closer together, stone seams crushed. A drainage channel she had measured three Tides ago no longer ran straight; it curved where the paving had sunk in a shallow arc. Cracks traced along the walls in a pattern that was not random. They bent toward a single node where load had once moved cleanly through the district. [file:adcc755e-2d4d-4e6c-9f5a-fade1acfde28][file:43bdf5f2-3f0e-4f8f-a81b-ece575e6600f]\r\n\r\nShe stood in the middle of the broken street with her logbook open, ink already drying in its last column from the previous Tide. The air still held fine dust and the metallic tang that had come with the Piercing. Her instruments were gone with the observatory, but she still had a straight-edge, a length of chalk, and her own eyes. [file:3f75f15a-1ea1-4a1a-afd2-6fe88e6bada9][file:c6e86552-223c-4995-a5d2-2b11adae00ca]\r\n\r\n“Ref point,” she murmured to herself. “Merro shop lintel, old level. Drop from there.” [file:3f75f15a-1ea1-4a1a-afd2-6fe88e6bada9]\r\n\r\nShe marked the old lintel height on the page from memory, then the new one from sight. The difference wasn’t much in span terms, but stone did not have to move far before it hurt people. She sketched the visible cracks, then overlaid the remembered map of main supports beneath the street. The lines did not match. They curved around a gap that had not existed before the white mark cut the sky. [file:0a2311af-b53e-4e20-9d0d-c7854ffa99a5][file:adcc755e-2d4d-4e6c-9f5a-fade1acfde28]\r\n\r\nShe turned a slow circle, counting under her breath. Every third crack showed the same faint copper shimmer she had seen under the building when the man with the shaking hands touched it. Those lines stopped abruptly, never reaching full contact lengths. Effective bearing zones, she wrote in the margin. Too small for safety. [file:9a4d8dfe-4d92-4951-b01e-4468bbe871ed][file:43bdf5f2-3f0e-4f8f-a81b-ece575e6600f]\r\n\r\n“Merrow!” [file:08b7f1e8-8b2b-47bf-b279-b9c04853c2ba]\r\n\r\nThe shout came from up-slope. Tiffani finished the last stroke of a crack and looked up. Two figures in guild vests picked their way through the rubble, followed by a man with the RRO’s blue sash at his shoulder. The sash was dusty but still neatly tied. The guild marks were hers: Merro Glass. She resisted the urge to wipe her own hands on her tunic before they saw how dirty they already were. [file:c6e86552-223c-4995-a5d2-2b11adae00ca][file:04c084e9-c169-4161-b5fe-2389183ec6ff]\r\n\r\n“Master Merrow,” the taller of the guild pair called again when he drew close enough not to shout. “You’re a hard woman to find.” [file:08b7f1e8-8b2b-47bf-b279-b9c04853c2ba]\r\n\r\n“Stones keep moving,” she said. “I keep following.” [file:3f75f15a-1ea1-4a1a-afd2-6fe88e6bada9]\r\n\r\nHe grimaced, glancing at the cracked facades and the copper-smudged seams. “Yes. About that.” He nodded toward the RRO man. “Inspector Harlen. Came to the guild. Asked for someone who can read breaks. We told him you were already in the mess.” [file:04c084e9-c169-4161-b5fe-2389183ec6ff][file:adcc755e-2d4d-4e6c-9f5a-fade1acfde28]\r\n\r\nHarlen stepped forward, hand on the edge of his sash, as if to remind them it was there. He took in her open logbook, the chalk smears on her fingers, and the line she had drawn from wall to street. His gaze paused for a breath on the copper glints along one crack. [file:c6e86552-223c-4995-a5d2-2b11adae00ca]\r\n\r\n“Master Merrow,” he said. “The RRO is assembling a committee to survey structural hazards post-Piercing. You were recommended. We would prefer your attention on that instead of...” He gestured loosely at her page. “Independent mapping.” [file:0a2311af-b53e-4e20-9d0d-c7854ffa99a5][file:04c084e9-c169-4161-b5fe-2389183ec6ff]\r\n\r\nTiffani shut the logbook halfway, thumb marking her place. “The city is already performing its own survey,” she said. “Cracks mark the map for us. I am only drawing what stone has decided.” [file:3f75f15a-1ea1-4a1a-afd2-6fe88e6bada9]\r\n\r\nHarlen’s jaw tightened at the word decided, but he let it pass. “The difference,” he said, “is that a coordinated effort can prioritize repairs. We need lists, not personal notebooks. The Crown wants numbers. How many supports compromised. How many households at risk. If you join formally, you will have resources. Assistants. Access to full plans.” [file:04c084e9-c169-4161-b5fe-2389183ec6ff][file:2db39d5a-8660-49cb-80cc-aacf669012c5]\r\n\r\n“And constraints,” she said. “Assignment orders. Approved routes.” [file:2db39d5a-8660-49cb-80cc-aacf669012c5]\r\n\r\n“Guidelines,” he corrected. “We must manage panic as much as stone. If every craftsman follows their own sense of urgency, we lose coherence. The RRO needs your precision, Master Merrow. In a framework.” [file:c6e86552-223c-4995-a5d2-2b11adae00ca]\r\n\r\nShe looked back at the nearest crack instead of his face. It bent away from where the official street plan claimed the main support should run. It bent toward the spot where the man with copper-gold hands had braced the wall the previous Tide. The heat there had not faded fully. Her palm still felt a slight temperature rise when she placed it on the stone. [file:9a4d8dfe-4d92-4951-b01e-4468bbe871ed]\r\n\r\n“You will prioritize by district,” she said. “By tax value. By how loud the Council screams.” [file:c6e86552-223c-4995-a5d2-2b11adae00ca]\r\n\r\nHarlen did not deny it. “We will prioritize where failure risks the greatest loss,” he said. “Load density, population, critical services. The Crown district feeds more mains than the Dregs. A collapse there—” [file:c2df7e75-e586-4cb9-b2d0-7cb6c5a2effe][file:04c084e9-c169-4161-b5fe-2389183ec6ff]\r\n\r\n“Kills different names,” she said. “Not more bodies.” [file:08b7f1e8-8b2b-47bf-b279-b9c04853c2ba]\r\n\r\nOne of the guild men shifted his weight. “Tiffani,” he said, dropping the formality. “The workshop needs you too. Orders backed up. People want replacement glazing, safe mirrors, anything that feels less broken than this.” He gestured at the street. “You could move your bench up-slope for a few Tides. Let the RRO handle the cracks and come back when the shouting dies down.” [file:f95a9974-0111-4675-b8e0-c475881d1b12][file:04c084e9-c169-4161-b5fe-2389183ec6ff]\r\n\r\nThere it was: the easier line. Make pretty things. Replace what smashed on other people’s heads. Pretend the pattern had not been visible in the glass before it broke. [file:3f75f15a-1ea1-4a1a-afd2-6fe88e6bada9]\r\n\r\nShe saw Marisse in her mind the way she had found her after that earlier failure, under a sheet of glass that had not been rated for the loads Tiffani had waved away as unlikely. The notes in her old logbook where she had written noise in the margin and turned the page. [file:08b7f1e8-8b2b-47bf-b279-b9c04853c2ba]\r\n\r\n“I have already watched one pattern until it killed someone,” she said. “I will not do that again because it is tidier for the Crown.” [file:08b7f1e8-8b2b-47bf-b279-b9c04853c2ba]\r\n\r\nHarlen’s expression cooled. “Refusing a formal role means you will have no say when we set priorities,” he said. “You can shout from the street, but decisions will be made in the chambers.” [file:c6e86552-223c-4995-a5d2-2b11adae00ca]\r\n\r\n“Then I will give you numbers from here,” she said. “You want lists? I will send lists. Holds, durations, estimated loads. Copper residue bands. You can stack them against your council minutes and see which one predicts who lives.” [file:43bdf5f2-3f0e-4f8f-a81b-ece575e6600f][file:9a4d8dfe-4d92-4951-b01e-4468bbe871ed]\r\n\r\nHe stared at her logbook, then back at her. “You are making this harder than it needs to be, Master Merrow.” [file:2db39d5a-8660-49cb-80cc-aacf669012c5]\r\n\r\n“It was hard the moment the sky split,” she said. “Everything since is just choosing where to stand.” [file:c6e86552-223c-4995-a5d2-2b11adae00ca]\r\n\r\nHarlen exhaled through his nose, a short, contained sound. “Very well. The offer stands if you change your mind. Send your lists to the RRO hall if you insist on working alone. We will—review them.” He did not quite promise to act. [file:04c084e9-c169-4161-b5fe-2389183ec6ff]\r\n\r\nHe nodded to the guild men and turned away, boots crunching on broken stone. They followed, one of them giving Tiffani a last, pleading look that said workshop, pay, simpler problems. She let it slide off. [file:f95a9974-0111-4675-b8e0-c475881d1b12]\r\n\r\nFor a few breaths, the street noise filled back in around her: hammer strikes, shouted measurements, a cart wheel complaining over a new ridge. She reopened the logbook and added a new header on a blank page. [file:3f75f15a-1ea1-4a1a-afd2-6fe88e6bada9]\r\n\r\nPost-Piercing Holds, she wrote. Source unknown. [file:9a4d8dfe-4d92-4951-b01e-4468bbe871ed]\r\n\r\nUnder it, she drew a narrow rectangle for the healer’s wall she had just inspected. She marked the hot band along Jhace’s touch line, shaded the cooler stone outside it, and noted effective support width less than half original span. Survived initial event. Unsafe for long-term occupancy. [file:43bdf5f2-3f0e-4f8f-a81b-ece575e6600f][file:9a4d8dfe-4d92-4951-b01e-4468bbe871ed]\r\n\r\nThen she turned the page again and began a table: Location, apparent load, visible copper, estimated hold duration, observed cost on subject. The last column stayed mostly blank for now. She had seen his hands shake and the vein-lines glow, but she did not yet have counts or Tides between use and failure. She meant to get them. [file:9a4d8dfe-4d92-4951-b01e-4468bbe871ed][file:2db39d5a-8660-49cb-80cc-aacf669012c5]\r\n\r\nPella found her three spans later, legs gray with dust up to the knee. Her apprentice carried two blankets and a satchel that bulged with wrapped bread and stoppered jars. [file:08b7f1e8-8b2b-47bf-b279-b9c04853c2ba]\r\n\r\n“You weren’t at the workshop,” Pella said without preamble. “So I followed the shouting. Also the runners saying ‘the glass woman with the book is down there.’ Eat.” She thrust a wrapped parcel toward Tiffani’s chest. [file:3f75f15a-1ea1-4a1a-afd2-6fe88e6bada9]\r\n\r\nTiffani caught it one-handed, barely saving her logbook from the same impact. “You are very polite today,” she said. “Did the world ending soften you?” [file:3f75f15a-1ea1-4a1a-afd2-6fe88e6bada9]\r\n\r\n“No,” Pella said. “The world ending made you forget to chew. Someone has to stop you from turning into a chalk sketch.” She glanced at the latest diagram. “That a new one?” [file:08b7f1e8-8b2b-47bf-b279-b9c04853c2ba]\r\n\r\n“Yes,” Tiffani said. “Healer’s wall. Held by our new variable.” She tapped the copper-shaded band. “Load concentrated along here. Temperature elevated. It should not stand with that little bearing length, but it does.” [file:9a4d8dfe-4d92-4951-b01e-4468bbe871ed]\r\n\r\nPella squinted. “New variable being the man with the light in his hands?” [file:08b7f1e8-8b2b-47bf-b279-b9c04853c2ba]\r\n\r\nTiffani broke the bread parcel open with a thumb, more for something to do than from hunger. “Yes,” she said. “Subject: Jhace Torrins. Underface inspector. Currently pretending his hands always shake.” [file:08b7f1e8-8b2b-47bf-b279-b9c04853c2ba][file:43bdf5f2-3f0e-4f8f-a81b-ece575e6600f]\r\n\r\nPella’s eyebrows rose. “You got his name.” [file:08b7f1e8-8b2b-47bf-b279-b9c04853c2ba]\r\n\r\n“He yelled it at a wall,” Tiffani said. “His friend yelled it louder. The city does not keep its miracles quiet.” [file:f95a9974-0111-4675-b8e0-c475881d1b12]\r\n\r\nShe took a bite. The bread tasted of ash and root, but it steadied her enough to keep talking.\r\n\r\n“He touches a failing point,” she said around the mouthful. “Copper-gold spreads along stress lines. Heat rises. For a short window, the structure behaves as if its failure thresholds moved. Not erased, just… shifted. Enough to let people flee.” She pointed with the crust at her table header. “I intend to know how long those windows last, and how narrow they are.” [file:9a4d8dfe-4d92-4951-b01e-4468bbe871ed]\r\n\r\nPella watched her for a breath. “So,” she said slowly. “The guild is asking you to polish glass for rich people. The RRO is asking you to sit at a table and write lists for them. And you want to chase a shaking man around broken streets and count how much he is breaking himself to keep other people alive.” [file:f95a9974-0111-4675-b8e0-c475881d1b12][file:2db39d5a-8660-49cb-80cc-aacf669012c5]\r\n\r\n“Yes,” Tiffani said. “That sums it.” [file:3f75f15a-1ea1-4a1a-afd2-6fe88e6bada9]\r\n\r\nPella huffed air that might have been a laugh if it had more strength. “Then I will bring more bread,” she said. “And extra ink. You are going to need both.” [file:08b7f1e8-8b2b-47bf-b279-b9c04853c2ba]\r\n\r\nShe set the second blanket on a low wall, then moved off to help a group of workers shift debris away from a still-occupied doorway. Tiffani watched her go, feeling the small knot in her chest that always eased when Pella was within sight. Then she bent back over the page. [file:08b7f1e8-8b2b-47bf-b279-b9c04853c2ba][file:f95a9974-0111-4675-b8e0-c475881d1b12]\r\n\r\nShe added a note at the bottom of the table: Hypothesis: effect tied to protective intention, not simple will. Activation only when lives in immediate danger. Cost accumulative in subject. Data incomplete. [file:9a4d8dfe-4d92-4951-b01e-4468bbe871ed]\r\n\r\nThe next hours blurred into a pattern of walk, measure, mark. She visited three more sites where witnesses swore they had seen copper-gold in the stone. At one, the residue had already cooled. At another, the glow still clung faintly, enough to make her skin prickle when she held her palm near it. Each time, she sketched the cracks, traced the probable load paths, and compared them to what she knew of standard construction. Each time, the numbers came back with the same message: the holds were inefficient, messy, and barely enough—and still better than collapse. [file:9a4d8dfe-4d92-4951-b01e-4468bbe871ed][file:adcc755e-2d4d-4e6c-9f5a-fade1acfde28]\r\n\r\nLate in Pouring, she claimed a corner of a mostly intact shop whose owner had given up selling anything that day and turned his counter into a writing surface. She spread her pages out, weighting corners with bits of broken masonry. Outside, the street still rumbled with carts and shouted orders. Inside, she had three paces of relative quiet. [file:adcc755e-2d4d-4e6c-9f5a-fade1acfde28][file:43bdf5f2-3f0e-4f8f-a81b-ece575e6600f]\r\n\r\nShe began listing patterns.\r\n\r\n- All confirmed holds occurred with people directly in harm’s path.\r\n- No holds recorded yet on empty structures.\r\n- Copper intensity correlated roughly with number of people saved, not with total building mass.\r\n- Subject cost shook her pen enough to dot the margin. [file:9a4d8dfe-4d92-4951-b01e-4468bbe871ed]\r\n\r\n“Not sustainable,” she said under her breath. “Not at that load.” [file:3f75f15a-1ea1-4a1a-afd2-6fe88e6bada9]\r\n\r\nShe circled the word cost three times.\r\n\r\nWhen she lifted her head, her neck ached and the light outside the doorway had shifted color. Voices in the street had changed note as well—from the sharp edges of immediate crisis to the flatter, strained marks of people settling into the idea that this was not ending in a single Tide. The grid’s hum under the floor still sat at its new, roughened pitch. [file:c2df7e75-e586-4cb9-b2d0-7cb6c5a2effe]\r\n\r\nShe closed the logbook and pressed the leather cover against her sternum for a breath. This, she thought, is the work. Not the committee rooms. Not the polished glass. Find the lines that do not lie and follow them, wherever they cut through guild comfort. [file:04c084e9-c169-4161-b5fe-2389183ec6ff][file:f95a9974-0111-4675-b8e0-c475881d1b12]\r\n\r\nWhen she stepped back into the street, her eyes went at once to the healer’s wall. Workers had added poles and wedges just where she had marked load risk earlier. Someone had listened to that much, at least. The crack still held its new lean. No fresh dust drifted from it. [file:adcc755e-2d4d-4e6c-9f5a-fade1acfde28]\r\n\r\nBeyond it, a small knot of people had gathered near another run entrance. Their attention all pointed the same direction. Tiffani followed their gaze. [file:43bdf5f2-3f0e-4f8f-a81b-ece575e6600f]\r\n\r\nJhace Torrins stood there with Bram at his shoulder.\r\n\r\nHe looked worse in full light than he had when he was pressed against the broken wall the previous Tide. The tremor in his hands showed even at this distance, fingers making small, constant corrections as if the air itself unbalanced them. His shoulders carried the set of someone who had already done more than his body approved of and planned to do more anyway. [file:08b7f1e8-8b2b-47bf-b279-b9c04853c2ba][file:9a4d8dfe-4d92-4951-b01e-4468bbe871ed]\r\n\r\nHe reached down for something at his feet—a tool, maybe, or a scrap of metal. He missed the first time. The second. On the third attempt he caught it, only to have it jolt free when his hand jumped. He straightened with his hands tucked behind his back, the motion too smooth to be unplanned. Bram’s face turned toward him with a look she recognized: concern wrapped in stubborn patience. [file:0a2311af-b53e-4e20-9d0d-c7854ffa99a5][file:08b7f1e8-8b2b-47bf-b279-b9c04853c2ba]\r\n\r\nTiffani flipped her logbook open again and made one last note on the cost column: Subject unable to perform fine grip on small object post-hold. Hiding symptom. [file:9a4d8dfe-4d92-4951-b01e-4468bbe871ed]\r\n\r\nThen she shut the book, tucked it under her arm, and started toward him.\r\n\r\nData, she thought. And choices. If the city insisted on growing stories around him, someone needed to anchor those stories to numbers before they led him into holds that would tear him apart. [file:43bdf5f2-3f0e-4f8f-a81b-ece575e6600f][file:2db39d5a-8660-49cb-80cc-aacf669012c5]\r\n\r\nShe walked with measured steps, counting cracks as she went. When she reached him, she would ask pointed questions. And this time, she would not let him dodge them with one-word answers and bad lies about old injuries."
          ]
        },
