          "parts": [
            "Perfect catches. I’ll make:\n\n- **“dropped a finger” → non-unit phrasing**\n- **“The tunnel answered” → “Another pulse hit”**\n- **Add visible vein-lines in the aftermath of the first hold**\n\nHere’s **locked Chapter 3** with those edits applied:\n\n---\n\n# Chapter 3: The Sky Breaks\n\nThe wall moved before Jhace did.\n\nStone shifted under his palm. A deep grind that did not belong. The tunnel’s thrum broke into a jagged pulse. The pipe beside him threw heat sideways hard enough that his fingers slipped.\n\nThen the ground hit.\n\nThe Underface lurched. Jhace slammed into the wall. His lantern broke free and swung wild on its hook. Bolts shrieked in their brackets. Somewhere upslope a main line roared as pressure slammed through it, a tearing sound that went through bone.\n\n“Off the ladders!” he shouted. “Now!”\n\nDust burst from the arch above, a blind cloud. The tunnel narrowed to sound and impact: stone cracking, metal twisting, grit falling in a hard rush that stung his eyes. He tasted old ash and something new under it, a sharp tang on his tongue.\n\n“Chloen!” Bram’s voice came from the ladder shaft. “Move!”\n\nJhace staggered toward the shaft by habit, one hand on the wall to keep direction. The grid vibration had turned uneven, rolling in waves. A brace ahead of him groaned. He wrapped both arms around it and braced his feet.\n\nThe first wave passed. The tunnel did not settle.\n\nAnother shudder rolled through. The arch above the shaft gave a hard, hollow crack.\n\n“Off!” Jhace bellowed. “Both of you, off that metal!”\n\nBoots hit stone. A heartbeat later the ladder’s top anchor ripped out of the arch. Iron rungs slammed against the wall, sparks flaring where they struck pipe. The whole frame came down in a rattle of metal, choking the shaft with noise.\n\nSomething heavier followed.\n\nA cross-beam tore loose from the upper structure and dropped through the open mouth of the shaft. Jhace saw only a bulked shadow in dust. It hit the tunnel floor with a wet, wrong sound. A short scream cut off.\n\nThe dust thinned enough to show Chloen under the beam.\n\nIt caught her across one leg above the knee. The rest of her lay clear. Her hands clawed at the floor. She dragged in air and let it out in a hoarse sound that barely reached words.\n\n“Jh—” She bit it off. Breath shook in her chest.\n\nBram skidded across stone, dropped to his knees at her side. Blood seeped under the beam’s edge, dark against the damp floor.\n\nJhace reached the timber in three strides. Thick, near his chest-width. One end splintered. The other still buried in ceiling rubble. The weight sat full on her leg.\n\nHe planted his feet, set both hands under the beam’s edge, and heaved.\n\nNothing moved.\n\nMuscle in his back lit with pain. The beam gave no answer. The arch above answered instead—another cracking line, the soft crumble of stone. If he kept forcing, he would bring the rest of the shaft down on all three of them.\n\n“Jhace,” Bram snapped. “Stop. You’ll bring the whole thing.”\n\nChloen’s fingers dug into the floor. She wasn’t making sound now. Her face had gone gray. Lips pulled back from her teeth.\n\n“How bad,” Jhace said.\n\n“Crushed,” Bram said. No comfort. “We get it off in the next few breaths, she keeps the limb. We fail, she bleeds out or the bone cuts through. Or it all comes down. Good choices.”\n\nAnother tremor rolled through. Smaller, but enough to rattle grit from the arch. The beam bounced once and settled heavier.\n\nChloen bit down on her sleeve. Her eyes found Jhace’s. Fear. And trust he had not earned.\n\nJhace saw another shaft in his head. A smaller brace. A quiet hiss he had called harmless. Twenty-three names after.\n\nNot again.\n\n“Bram,” he said. “Pull when I tell you. Straight back. Keep her spine clear.”\n\n“Jhace—”\n\n“Now or we lose her.”\n\nBram moved. He slid his arms under Chloen’s shoulders and upper body and planted his boots wide.\n\nJhace spat dust, wiped his palms once on his trousers, and put both hands on the beam again. This time he did not try to lift. He let skin settle to rough wood. He felt the crushed fibers where impact had flattened grain. He dropped one knee to stone and lowered his center.\n\nThe old grid thrum still ran under everything. A sharper pulse rode on top of it.\n\nHe fixed on the weight at one point.\n\nHold. Long enough.\n\nHeat built in his palms. At first it felt like friction. Then it sharpened and sank through skin into bone. The pressure under his hands changed. Mass turned into resistance. Something pushed back.\n\nCopper-gold light spilled out around his fingers.\n\nJhace flinched and did not pull away. The light came from his own skin, thin streams running into the wood grain. It spread into the shattered end and into the stone on either side, tracing stress he did not know how to name. The beam vibrated with it, a tight resonance inside the deeper shake of the tunnel.\n\nHis arms locked. Shoulder to jaw clenched. Breath left and did not come back clean.\n\n“Now,” he got out.\n\nBram hauled. The beam did not rise much. It did lighten. Jhace felt the load shift through his hands, up his forearms, into his chest, then out again. Sweat ran into his eyes. His teeth ached. For a few breaths the world narrowed to the hot line where flesh met wood.\n\nThen Chloen slid free.\n\nHer leg came out from under the beam in a rush. It looked wrong—swollen, skin split, blood running—but not as much bone under skin as he expected. Copper-gold traces ran across the bruised flesh for a heartbeat, then sank out of sight.\n\nShe screamed once and sagged against Bram’s hold, panting.\n\n“Got you,” Bram said, voice rough. “Got you. Stay with me.”\n\nJhace let go.\n\nThe copper-gold snapped out. The weight threw itself back into the wood. The beam dropped and jolted the stone, then settled, sending a shallow crack through the floor but no full collapse.\n\nPain hit his hands. Not a cut. A wide burn from fingertips to elbow. His fingers curled without consent. Tremor ran through both arms. Copper-gold lines followed the veins in his forearms for a breath, then faded, leaving only the shake.\n\n“What,” Bram said. He stared at Jhace’s hands. “What did you—”\n\nAnother pulse hit.\n\nA deeper shock rolled through the Underface. Not a tremor. A single, enormous surge. The tunnel lights flared and went out. Pipe noise jumped in pitch, then steadied at a harsh new register. A low roar came from everywhere at once—stone and air and the open shaft above.\n\nThe air pressed inward for several breaths, then snapped out again.\n\nJhace hit the floor on one knee. Pain in his hands fought with a heavy pressure in his chest that did not belong to breath.\n\nHe looked up.\n\nThrough the shaft mouth he saw the sky. Not blue. A slice of white so bright it hurt, cutting across the visible strip. Color at its edges warped. Rooftop outlines bent. Fine dust rained down, glittering faintly copper in the light.\n\nThen rubble slid across the gap and the shaft choked.\n\nHe heard screams above. Not one street. Many.\n\n“Out,” Jhace said. The word scraped his throat. “We get out.”\n\n“Chloen can’t climb,” Bram said.\n\n“Then we carry.”\n\nJhace forced his hands open. Pain flared fresh. Tremor kept going.\n\n“Emergency hatch,” he said. “Two junctions river-side. That run ties to street.”\n\nBram shifted Chloen’s weight across his shoulders. Her injured leg hung wrong, but when it bumped against his chest she didn’t scream. She hissed and clamped her jaw, eyes unfocused.\n\nJhace pushed up. The tremor in his hands made the stone edges blur. He set his shoulder to the beam as they passed, testing. It felt stable enough for the breaths it would take to clear.\n\nThey ran.\n\nThe tunnel had changed shape. Some braces had shifted. Some chalk marks lay under fresh dust. Twice they climbed over fallen pipe. Once a side wall buckled inward, leaving a narrow gap. Jhace shoved at broken stone until it gave them passage, ignoring the way his palms complained.\n\nBy the time they reached the hatch, his breath tore in his chest. The metal door had jammed in its frame. He set both hands to it and shoved.\n\nFor a breath, a faint copper-gold shimmer traced the hinges. Heat stung up his forearms. The frame groaned and moved. Pain sharpened in his palms.\n\nBram got a boot into the gap and kicked it wider.\n\nThey spilled onto the surface.\n\nThe street looked wrong. Stone fractured in long, uneven lines. A building front across the way had peeled open, its face sheared away, rooms exposed. Dust drifted in slow curtains. People ran in broken patterns, some toward the damage, some away.\n\nAbove all of it, far higher than any tower, the white line cut the sky.\n\nFrom here he could see more of it. It did not fall. It passed overhead, impossibly long, light bending along its edges. Fine metallic dust hung in its wake, catching light in a faint copper shimmer that clung to the air.\n\nJhace had no word for it. He had work.\n\nThe building to their right groaned. The lower corner had sunk. Its load had shifted onto a single main support near where street met wall. A knot of people huddled at its base, half in the doorway, half in the open.\n\n“Back!” Jhace shouted. “Away from that wall!”\n\nSome obeyed. Some didn’t. A woman with glass dust in her hair stood near the edge of the crowd, turned upward, staring. A logbook clutched to her chest. Jhace did not know her name.\n\nThe support post split with a harsh crack. Stone above it began to fold. Blocks shifted in a chain, strain passing down the line.\n\nIf that post went, the whole corner followed. The doorway crushed anyone still within reach. Too many still stood too close.\n\nJhace’s hands burned. His fingers shook. His body already felt used past what it was meant to do.\n\nNot again.\n\nHe ran for the failing post.\n\n“Jhace!” Bram shouted behind him. “You can’t—”\n\nJhace dropped to his knees at the base of the support, where stone met foundation. Hairline fractures spidered out from the contact point. Dust leaked in slow streams. He pressed both palms flat against the cracked surface. Skin to stone.\n\nHold. Long enough for them to run.\n\nHeat surged out of him. Copper-gold burst around his fingers, brighter than before. It drove into the fractures, tracing each line through the stone. From his hands it spread upward into the post and sideways into adjoining blocks, chasing stress and filling it with glow.\n\nFor a breath he felt every load path. Every place weight had shifted. It was too much. The sense of connection ran from his palms into shoulder and chest and down his spine. Vibration filled his skull.\n\nHe dug his fingers into stone. Forearms locked. Breath turned shallow and ragged.\n\n“Move!” Bram bellowed. “Everyone away! Now!”\n\nFeet pounded broken street. The crowd pulled back. The woman with the logbook staggered but did not fall. Her gaze fixed on the glow around his hands, wide and sharp. Glass shards at her feet picked up the copper-gold tint and hummed.\n\nThe building’s lean slowed.\n\nThe main support still wanted to fail. Cracks pressed against his hands. Stone above tried to fold further. He pushed back with body and heat and will, nothing clean about it.\n\nHis vision narrowed at the edges. Sound dulled. Copper-gold brightened until it washed detail away. Only masses and lines remained.\n\n“Jhace,” Bram’s voice came from far off. “That’s enough. They’re clear. Let it go.”\n\nJhace tried to answer. His jaw would not move.\n\nHe held for three more heartbeats.\n\nThen his arms stopped obeying.\n\nThe light cut out. The support dropped a small distance, then caught on a new, broken balance. Stone dust cascaded in a fresh curtain. A few blocks fell and shattered in the empty doorway. The building groaned and settled into a shape that did not crush anyone.\n\nJhace tipped sideways.\n\nHe hit the street on his shoulder. His hands twitched against stone. Tremor ran uncontrolled from fingertips to elbow, violent and fine. Pain bloomed deep and throbbing.\n\nThe sky tilted. The white line overhead had already passed, leaving haze.\n\nEverything darkened.\n\n—\n\nSound came back first. Shouts. Weeping. The ring of tools. Someone calling orders. The grid’s low thrum, altered but present.\n\nThen weight: a rough blanket over his chest, stone under his back.\n\nThen his hands. Still shaking.\n\nJhace opened his eyes.\n\nHe lay on broken street, propped against fallen masonry. Dust coated his clothes. Copper-gold smears marked the stone near his hands, fading by the breath. His fingers jerked in small, useless motions.\n\nA shadow crossed his face.\n\nA woman stood over him. Dark hair pulled back in a knot, streaked with fine glass dust and copper shimmer. A logbook clenched to her chest. Her eyes moved over him in quick passes: hands, arms, the stone behind his back, the faint traces of glow still seeping into cracks he had held.\n\nShock sat on her face. Behind it, calculation.\n\n“What did you do?” she asked.\n\nHer voice held no awe. Only demand.\n\nJhace swallowed. His throat scraped. His hands would not stop moving.\n\n“I held,” he said. “It should have fallen.”\n\nTheir gazes locked for a breath. The city groaned around them. The grid’s hum ran at a new pitch under the noise.\n\nThe world had changed. His body knew it. So did her eyes. Neither of them had a name for any of it yet."
          ]
        },

    "7e8ce07a-2ea6-47c3-a9e9-251dc6a00d9e": {
      "children": [
        "a25fdd1c-c7c2-4e7b-af4f-d17daf88865b"
      ],
      "id": "7e8ce07a-2ea6-47c3-a9e9-251dc6a00d9e",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": null,
          "role": "assistant"
        },
        "channel": "final",
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "Got it — I’ll keep **800–1300 words** as the target window for each chapter.\n\nHere’s **Chapter 1** revised and tightened to sit roughly in that range:\n\n---\n\n### Chapter 1 – Something Wrong  \n\nThe tunnel hummed around Jhace, a low steady note in stone and copper.\n\nHe stood with one palm on the main pipe, the other on the wall, breath held. Heat pressed against his skin. The copper line under his hand shivered with flow from the deep wells up-slope, pushing toward homes and forges above.\n\nStrong. Continuous.\n\nHe opened his eyes and marked the junction anyway.\n\n“Marry point seven-two, brace intact, flange seating clean,” he muttered. Chalk scratched against stone as he wrote. He checked the bolt heads by habit, running a thumb over each to feel for rounding from stress. Clean edges. No movement.\n\nHe moved on.\n\nThe tunnel ahead narrowed to single file. Thick mains at knee and chest, smaller thimble-width lines bent over them in tight curves. Condensation beaded along every run and dropped in slow rhythm to the floor, patterning the stone in dark circles.\n\nJhace walked heel to toe, feeling each contact. The grid spoke there too: faint vibration through his boots, through bones, up into his spine. This section’s tone sat in his memory from countless routes. No surprises yet.\n\nHe passed a ladder well. Light from a grate above fell in a thin shaft, bright against the tunnel gloom. Voices dropped through with it, warped by echo.\n\n“…tell him he can log it later, Bram, we’re already behind…”\n\nChloen.\n\nJhace did not look up. He knew the layout above by sound and smell. The short stretch of forge corridor, Bram’s portable furnace at the far wall, the water cask always wedged near the ladder, the open grate that never sealed quite right. Forge smoke drifted down, carrying metal tang and stew.\n\nHe also knew they were waiting for him to climb up and eat.\n\nLater.\n\nHe lifted his lamp and ducked under a low cross-run, shoulders rolling to clear the tight space. Sweat dampened the back of his shirt; the air ran hot here, close to a main riser. Beneath the pipe noise and distant city rumble, he listened for the only thing that mattered.\n\nCracks did not shout. They changed the song. A clean line hummed. A damaged one caught, hissed, turned smooth vibration rough. Once you knew the sound of a section, you could hear when it shifted.\n\nHe turned into the next junction and felt it before he heard it.\n\nA double beat in the floor. A tiny uneven catch in the hum.\n\nHe stopped mid-step. The lamp swung, throwing bands of light over pipes and brackets. Nothing in front of him looked wrong. The main ran straight, regular brackets holding it to the wall. Welds at the joints were smooth. No scorch marks, no discoloration. The air felt even.\n\nHe set the lamp down on a dry patch of floor and crouched. Both hands on the main.\n\nHeat met skin again, stronger at this depth. Flow pushed steadily toward the Crown districts. He kept his breath even and counted heartbeats.\n\nOne, two, three, four—\n\nThere. A fraction of a heartbeat’s lag between pulses under his left hand and his right. The pipe itself sat under even pressure; that was not the source. Something in the stone cradle under the bracket, or in the arch above, carried load late.\n\n“Of course,” he breathed.\n\nHe shifted his touch to the bracket, fingers tracing the join between copper and stone. No hairline fractures. No chipped edges. He leaned one shoulder into the wall, closed his eyes partway, and listened through bone.\n\nThe hum here sat off-key from the tunnel’s usual tone. Not by much. Enough.\n\nAbove, Bram’s voice cut down through the ladder shaft.\n\n“Jhace! You still courting that grid? Quench will come before you’re done.”\n\nCloser, Chloen’s: “Should I go down and see—”\n\n“Stay where you are,” Jhace called, not lifting his head. Sound carried clean along the stone. “If the brace fails and you’re on the ladder, the pipe pins you to the wall. That is a bad story.”\n\nSilence, then Bram’s amused rumble.\n\n“Hear that, Chloen? Romance. Nothing says ‘good mid-Tide’ like a lecture on crushing.”\n\nChloen laughed. Jhace kept his shoulder against the stone.\n\nMain line, stable. No visible deformation. Bracket sound. No seep from joints. Temperature even. The wrong note could come from a gate up-slope, a new draw opening, anything that shifted flow patterns. This did not feel like that. The cadence bothered him. Too irregular.\n\nHe took the tap bar from his belt pouch and struck the brace three times. Clear ringing through stone, clean decay.\n\nPerfect, on paper.\n\nHis gut still complained.\n\nThe guild liked numbers and tables. Load limits. Rated spans. No form for “uneasy bones.”\n\nTwenty-two Turns of routes said something else. When your body said a structure was off, it usually was.\n\nHe pressed his ear to the wall and stretched one arm along the pipe, palm flat.\n\nTunnel hum spread wider through him this way—from skull to chest to hand. He listened past his own breath for a pattern that did not belong.\n\nIt surfaced. Every tenth pulse, a slight stutter. Not large. Just enough for pressure to slip somewhere it should not.\n\nEasy enough to shrug. One junction out of hundreds. No crack to show. If he logged it as anomaly, someone up-slope would see his name and complain about delays, extra work, wasted fuel.\n\nShut the section down, Bram would say, and forges go cold. Kitchens curse. Management calls him in again.\n\nWhat if he did nothing?\n\nHe did not need imagination for that answer. Only memory: a different tunnel, smaller mains, his arms shorter, hands softer. A faint hiss he did not know how to read. A supervisor who said it was regular pipe chatter. A Tide later, a building above failed. Twenty-three bodies out of rubble. Two called Torrins.\n\nHe pulled back from the wall. The wrong hum still lay under the regular song, steady and patient.\n\nJhace picked up his chalk.\n\n“Marry point seven-three,” he murmured. The chalk squeaked. “Vibration anomaly. No visible fault. Recheck before next Tide. Recommend partial shutdown and brace reinforcement.”\n\nHe underlined “recommend.” It would still land badly, but it was the truth he could live with.\n\n“Jhace?” Chloen again. “You all right down there?”\n\n“I’m breathing,” he said. “You?”\n\n“Bored,” she answered, then quickly, “Bracing supports, the ones you marked.”\n\n“She’s bracing,” Bram added. “And pestering. Come up. Eat before the stew hardens.”\n\nJhace wiped sweat from his temple with the back of his wrist. His stomach tightened at the smell drifting down through the shaft.\n\n“Two spans,” he said. “I want the next junction.”\n\n“He says two spans,” Bram told Chloen, voice fading as he turned away. “So we’ll see him by Quench.”\n\nThe hammer rhythm above resumed. Chloen’s lighter steps moved between brace points. The forge became steady noise again.\n\nJhace took up his lamp and walked.\n\nThe tunnel bent river-side, following the city’s slope. Old chalk marks covered the walls: circles where cracks once sat, crosses on replaced sections, initials from other inspectors. He paused at each, palm to metal, but all those repairs held steady. Their tones matched his memory.\n\nAt the next junction, he ran his sequence. Visual. Tap. Hand on pipe. Ear to stone.\n\nNothing obvious. No seep, no hairline in the bracket. Tap ring clean, maybe even prettier than the last.\n\nWhen he laid his fingers on the metal, the hum still felt wrong.\n\nHe set the lamp down, stepped back, and planted his boots wide on the stone. One hand on the wall, one on the pipe.\n\nThen stillness.\n\nHeat from the main. Duller vibration from secondary lines. Deeper, slower tremor from buried arches behind the visible work.\n\nThe off note from the last junction sat here too. Same count. Every tenth pulse, the same small catch.\n\nThis span of tunnel should have had a different load mix. Different draws, different branches. The match bothered him more than any crack would have.\n\n“Could be a deep valve cycling,” he told the tunnel. Saying it did not make it feel true. Valve chains had their own distinctive patterns.\n\nHe logged this point as well. His handwriting tightened.\n\n“Pattern repeat at seven-four. Same cadence. Source unknown.”\n\nHe snapped the chalk tip by pressing too hard and cursed under his breath. Another stub came out of the pouch.\n\nHe considered climbing up now. Tell Bram there was a pattern. Ask him to start shutting sections, maybe wider than they usually risked. He could hear Bram’s sigh already, long and theatrical.\n\n“You don’t close a third of the Underface in Kindling because your skin itches,” Bram would say. Then he would look at Jhace’s face and soften, but the fight with management would still come.\n\nYou do not close for a feeling.\n\nYou do mark it.\n\nJhace moved again.\n\nThe air cooled and grew more damp where the mains swelled in diameter to feed both Underface and Glass Quarter. The brackets here were newer, etched with Bram’s tidy marks. Good work, tied into more stable stone.\n\nHe put his hand on one of those new runs.\n\nThe same stutter answered.\n\nThis time, the wrong pulse hit harder. The lamp flame trembled though no draft moved. Dust loosened from the arch and drifted down. Every bracket gave a faint metallic tick against its bolts.\n\nThe pipe under his hand no longer just hummed. A deeper vibration rolled through it, from every direction at once, from floor and wall and ceiling together.\n\nJhace froze.\n\nFor a heartbeat he could not tell which way led up-slope. The sensation had no single source. The city’s bones clenched, then tried to settle. They did not find their old rest.\n\nThen the extra note faded. The grid’s usual hum returned: hot, alive, familiar.\n\nHis own heart beat too fast.\n\nHe took his hand off the pipe and found his fingers locked hard into the leather of his glove. He flexed them slowly. No pain, no tremor. Just tension.\n\nHe lifted the chalk one more time.\n\n“City-wide tremor,” he wrote. “Not grid. Origin unknown. Recheck before next Tide.”\n\nToo vague. Too weak. It was also all he had.\n\nHe blew dust off the fresh mark and stepped back, lamp raised, listening.\n\nThe heat-grid still held. The tunnels still stood. His route still lay ahead, paces and paces of stone and copper waiting for his hands.\n\nSomething had shifted under Saint Merro. He could not name it. He could not fix it.\n\nNot yet.\n\nJhace picked up his route again, boots steady on the stone, every sense tuned to a hum that no longer felt like simple pipe-song. The structure of his city had changed. His body would not let him pretend otherwise."
          ]
        },

    "7ecc96a6-3ada-400f-83cf-37d58900df90": {

        "f8ee7f4f-3c42-4e3b-bd88-eb16df3d5231"
      ],
      "id": "7ecc96a6-3ada-400f-83cf-37d58900df90",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": null,
          "role": "assistant"
        },
        "channel": "final",
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "### Chapter 1 – Something Wrong  \n\nThe tunnel hummed around Jhace, a steady low note in stone and copper.\n\nHe stood still and let it run through him. One palm on the main pipe, one hand on the wall, breath held. Heat pressed against his skin. The copper line under his fingers shivered with flow from the deep wells inland, up through the Underface and toward the homes and forges above.\n\nGood. Strong. Continuous.\n\nHe opened his eyes and marked the junction anyway.\n\n“Marry point seven-two, junction brace intact, flange seating clean,” he muttered, chalk scratching his notation onto the stone. He checked the bolt heads by habit, a quick scrape of his thumb to feel for any rounding from stress. Clean edges. No movement.\n\nHe moved on.\n\nThe tunnel ahead pinched down to single file. Pipes stacked over one another, thick mains at knee and chest, smaller thimble-width lines bent over them in tight curves. Condensation beaded along every run. Drops fell in slow rhythm onto the floor, darkening the stone in small circles.\n\nJhace walked with his usual economical gait, heel to toe, feeling each contact. The grid spoke through those points too: faint vibration through boots, through legs, up into his spine. Each section had its familiar tone. Years of routes had carved them into his body.\n\nHe passed a ladder well. Light from a grate above dropped in a thin shaft, bright against the tunnel gloom. Voices came with it, warbled by the echo.\n\n“…tell him he can log it later, Bram, we’re already behind…”\n\nChloen.\n\nJhace did not look up. He knew the exact position of the surface street by the way the sound bounced, by the smell of forge smoke drifting down from Bram’s work level. He knew where Bram’s portable furnace sat, where the water cask always rested against the wall near the ladder.\n\nHe also knew they were both waiting for him to climb up for food.\n\nLater.\n\nHe lifted his lamp higher and ducked under a low cross-run, shoulders rolling through the tight space. Sweat already dampened the back of his shirt; the air here ran hot, close to one of the main risers. Above the pipe noise and the distant rumble of the city, he listened for the thing that mattered.\n\nCracks did not speak in the way fresh metal did. They hissed. They snagged the flow, turned smooth notes into something rough. Once you learned the clean sound of a line, you could hear when it shifted.\n\nHe turned into the next junction and felt it before he heard it.\n\nA double beat in the floor. A small, uneven catch in the hum.\n\nHe stopped mid-step. The lamp swung, throwing moving bands of light over pipes and brackets. Nothing in front of him looked wrong. The main ran straight, supported on regular stone brackets. Welds at the joints were smooth. The air temperature felt consistent across the span of the wall.\n\nHe put the lamp down, kneeling to set it on a dry patch of floor. Then he crouched and laid both hands on the main.\n\nHeat met skin again, stronger at this depth. Flow pressed along his palms, a constant push toward the Crown district. He kept his breath even and counted heartbeats.\n\nOne, two, three, four—\n\nThere. Slight lag between pulses on his left hand and right. The pipe was under even pressure; that wasn’t it. The lag came from the stone cradle under the bracket, or the arch above, or both. A fraction of a heartbeat, barely there, but present.\n\n“Of course,” he said under his breath. “Nothing simple.”\n\nHe shifted his hands to the bracket, fingers tracing the join between copper and stone. No hairline fractures, no spalling, no chipped edges. He leaned his shoulder into the wall, eyes half closed, and listened through bone.\n\nThe hum here was off the tunnel’s usual key. Not catastrophic. But not right.\n\nUp through the ladder shaft, Bram’s voice dropped in, muffled.\n\n“Jhace! You still courting that grid? Quench will come before you’re done down there.”\n\nThen, closer to the opening, Chloen’s younger tone: “Should I go down and see—”\n\n“Stay where you are,” Jhace called, without raising his head. His voice carried easily in the confined space. “If the brace goes and you’re on the ladder, you get crushed between pipe and wall. That’s a foolish way to die.”\n\nSilence above for a few breaths. Then Bram’s amused rumble.\n\n“Hear that, Chloen? Romantic, this one. Nothing says ‘good mid-Tide to you’ like a vivid description of your own crushing.”\n\nChloen laughed. Jhace ignored it, focusing back on the wall.\n\nHe tried to talk himself through it.\n\nMain line, stable. No visible deformation. Support brace holding. No seep from joints. Temperature even. The wrong note could come from load changes further up-slope—someone shutting a gate, a new draw opening—but the cadence felt wrong even for that. Too irregular.\n\nHe reached into his belt pouch and pulled out the thin metal tap bar. Three sharp strikes along the brace sent clear ringing through the stone. He listened to the decay. Sharp start, clean tail. On paper, perfect.\n\nHis gut still complained.\n\nHe hated that. The guild liked numbers, tables, rated loads. Gut didn’t sit neatly in a ledger. But twenty-two Turns of listening to structures had taught him one thing: when the body said something was wrong, it usually was.\n\nHe tried another test. He pressed his ear to the wall and stretched one arm along the pipe, palm flat.\n\nThe hum spread wider through him this way, from ear to shoulder to chest to hand. He listened past his own breath, past the faint shift of fabric, for beating that did not match memory.\n\nA pattern emerged. Every tenth pulse, a stutter. Not large. Just enough for pressure to slip somewhere it should not.\n\nIt would be easy to shrug. One junction out of hundreds. No crack he could point to. If he logged this as an anomaly, someone up-slope would see his name on the report and mutter about overcaution again. About wasted spans and wasted fuel.\n\nShut the section down, Bram would say, and the forges above would go cold. The kitchens would curse his name. Management would call him in for another talk.\n\nWhat if he did not shut it down?\n\nHe did not need imagination for the answer. He had memory.\n\nA different tunnel, smaller pipes, a younger Jhace with shorter arms and less scar on his hands. A subtle hiss he did not know how to read. A supervisor who told him it was fine, that metal warped and rattled and that was just the way of things. A Tide later, the building over that section had failed. Twenty-three bodies pulled from the stone and rubble. Two of them his parents.\n\nHe opened his eyes and pulled back from the wall. The wrong hum was still there, patient and steady.\n\nJhace picked up his chalk again.\n\n“Marry point seven-three,” he muttered. The chalk squeaked. “Unresolved vibration anomaly. Secondary inspection required. Recommend partial shutdown and brace reinforcement before next Pouring.”\n\nHe underlined “recommend” twice. That would not make it easier to sell. But if something failed here and he had not marked it, the fault would be his. He could not leave it unlogged and still walk through the tunnels without feeling every bracket on his skin.\n\n“Jhace?” Chloen again. “You all right down there?”\n\n“I’m breathing,” he said. “You?”\n\n“Bored,” she called. Then, quickly, “Bracing the supports you told me to, I mean.”\n\n“She’s bracing,” Bram added. “And pestering. Come up. We have food before it turns from stew to paste.”\n\nJhace wiped sweat from his temple with the back of his wrist. His stomach reminded him it existed. The smell of whatever Bram had put in the pot drifted faint and tempting through the shaft.\n\n“Two spans,” Jhace said. “I want to clear to the next junction.”\n\n“He says two spans,” Bram told Chloen, voice fading slightly as he turned away. “So we’ll see him by Quench. Maybe.”\n\nThe ladder rattled as Bram moved. Footfalls on the boards above settled back into the usual forge rhythm. Hammer, anvil, the sharp exhale Bram always made when lifting something heavy. Jhace pictured Chloen mimicking his hand placements, rechecking the braces topside, trying to earn approval she already had.\n\nHe picked up his lamp and moved on.\n\nThe tunnel turned toward the river-side, following the lay of the city. He passed old chalk marks left by other inspectors: circles around past cracks, crosses marking replaced sections, initials that meant little to him. He paused at each out of habit, pressing a hand to the metal there, but all of them sang steady now. Repairs had held.\n\nHis route for the Tide would take him out toward a cluster of smaller mains, then back along a different level. Plenty of spans left to travel before he called the inspection complete. Plenty of potential failures between here and the next ladder.\n\nHe reached the next junction brace and started his usual sequence. Visual. Tap. Hand on pipe. Ear to stone.\n\nVisually, nothing. Tap ring: clean. But when he put his fingers on the metal, the hum felt slightly different again.\n\nNo. Not just here.\n\nHe straightened and set the lamp down carefully. For a breath he did nothing at all. Then he spread his stance, feet apart on the stone floor, and placed one hand against the wall on his left, one on the pipe to his right.\n\nHe went still.\n\nThe grid’s voice rolled through him, all of it at once. Heat from the main, the duller vibration of secondary lines, the deeper, slower tremor from structural arches buried behind the visible work.\n\nThe off note he had heard at the last junction was present here too.\n\nHe swallowed. The distance between the two points was more than a few paces. This should have been a different microclimate, a different combination of loads. But the stutter fell in the same count. Every tenth pulse. The same tiny catch.\n\nHe let go and stepped away.\n\n“Could be a deep main valve cycling,” he told the empty tunnel. Saying it aloud did not make it feel truer. Valves had their own pattern. This felt…not that. The stutter was not localized to the heat-grid at all. It felt underneath. Beneath even the old footings.\n\nHe did not have language for vibration that ran through the city itself.\n\nHe logged the second anomaly anyway, chalk scrawl tighter this time.\n\n“Pattern repeat at seven-four. Same cadence. Source unknown,” he wrote. His hand pressed too hard; the chalk tip snapped. He cursed under his breath and fished another piece from his pouch.\n\nHe considered climbing up to Bram, telling him to shut down the whole section of grid, maybe wider. He could already hear Bram’s sigh, the long, suffering one he used for the guild’s more absurd demands.\n\n“Shut down a third of the Underface in Kindling because the pipes feel strange” would qualify as absurd.\n\nBut Jhace knew the other sound Bram made, the quieter one: the sound of anger when preventable harm happened. Bram would back him if he had a specific failure to point at. Not a feeling.\n\n“You don’t shut a city for a feeling,” he told himself.\n\nYou do log it.\n\nHe forced himself to keep moving, one junction at a time. Each point he checked carried the same signature. Stable on the surface, wrong underneath. By the fourth repeat, the hum had settled into his bones enough that he did not need to listen in detail; he could tell by the way his teeth faintly buzzed.\n\nHe hated that, too.\n\nAt the next ladder shaft, he paused. Light fell down in the same narrow band. He could hear Bram’s hammer, Chloen’s softer movements, the clink of tools.\n\nHe should eat. He should drink water that was not warmed by pipe heat. He should tell them what he was hearing.\n\nInstead, he stepped past the ladder and kept walking.\n\nThe tunnel air changed. Less forge smoke, more cold stone and faint river damp. The mains here had larger diameters, feeding both Underface and Glass Quarter. The brackets were newer, installed in the last few Tides, with Bram’s markings etched into the metal. Strong work.\n\nHe set his hand on one of those new sections.\n\nThe same wrong stutter answered.\n\nThis time, when it hit, the entire tunnel seemed to shift by a fraction of a grain. The lamp flame quivered without a draft. Dust loosened from the ceiling and drifted downward in a low cloud. The pipe under his hand did not just hum; it vibrated with a new, deeper note that did not belong to any system he knew.\n\nIt came from every direction at once.\n\nJhace froze. For a heartbeat, he could not tell which way was up-slope or river-side. The sensation had no source. Walls, floor, ceiling, pipes, brackets—everything carried the same pulse, as if the city had clenched and then tried to relax again but failed to reach rest.\n\nThe stutter passed. The tunnel settled. The usual grid hum resumed, regular and familiar.\n\nHis own heart beat too fast in his chest.\n\nHe took his hand off the pipe and realized his fingers had dug into the leather of his glove hard enough to crease it. He flexed them slowly. No pain, no tremor. Stage zero, his mind supplied in old training shorthand, and then he dismissed the thought. There was no magic here, only heat and stone.\n\nHe lifted the chalk once more and hesitated. How did you write what he had just felt?\n\n“City-wide tremor, non-grid, origin unknown,” he finally scratched onto the wall. “Pattern does not match known valve or pressure cycles. Recheck before next Tide.”\n\nNot enough. Too vague. Too weak. But it was what he had.\n\nHe blew dust off the fresh mark and stepped back, lamp held high, listening to the tunnels breathe.\n\nSomething had woken under Saint Merro. Or above it. Or both.\n\nHe could not name it. He couldn’t fix it. Yet.\n\nJhace picked up his route again, boots steady on the stone, every sense tuned to the hum that no longer felt familiar. The grid still held. For now.\n\nBut the structure of the world had shifted, and his bones knew it. fileciteturn1file11 fileciteturn0file10 fileciteturn0file0"
          ]
        },
