    "eaa62f8f-f8aa-4d1e-8349-81667fe0fea4": {
      "children": [
        "941baa59-80ad-48bf-b4f3-542b609467e7"
      ],
      "id": "eaa62f8f-f8aa-4d1e-8349-81667fe0fea4",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": null,
          "role": "assistant"
        },
        "channel": "final",
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "## Chapter 5: Lines That Don’t Lie  \n\nTiffani rebuilt her map before she trusted her eyes.\n\nThe kiln corridor still existed in the way the Crown would insist it did—names, taxes, shop marks, routes for carts—but the stone had shifted in ways that made those labels unreliable. Two fronts that used to leave a clear pace between their thresholds now pressed closer, their shared seam crushed and dusted white. A drainage channel she had measured three Tides earlier no longer ran straight; it bent where the paving had sunk in a shallow arc. The bend mattered. Water followed bends, and so did rot, and so did frost-crack when Quench turned sharp.\n\nShe stood in the middle of the broken street with her logbook open against her forearm. The air still held grit and the metallic tang that had followed the sky’s white mark. Her observatory was gone—stair torn, platform split, its instruments taken by gravity and glass shatter—and she felt its absence as missing reference points. She had replaced them with a straight-edge, a stub of chalk, and the habit of verifying everything twice.\n\n“Reference,” she murmured. “Merrow lintel. Old height.”\n\nShe faced her family’s shop and fixed her gaze on the lintel beam above the doorway. The beam itself had survived, but the wall around it had not. She marked the old line from memory in her book, then sketched the new line by sight. The difference was small. Stone did not have to move far before it broke ribs.\n\nShe stepped to the nearest crack and dragged chalk along it, forcing the line to show where dust tried to hide it. The crack ran up, then angled, then stopped at a seam that should have carried load cleanly into the next block. It did not. It diverted toward a node that used to sit quiet beneath the street.\n\nShe turned slowly, counting cracks under her breath and marking every third one that carried the same faint copper shimmer. Those lines stopped abruptly, never reaching the full contact lengths that a real support wanted. She wrote in the margin: **effective bearing zone too narrow**. Under it: **residue persists**.\n\nIt was the same pattern she had felt under her palm the previous Tide when Jhace Torrins pressed his shaking hands into a wall and forced it to hold long enough for the wounded to be dragged clear. The stone at that touch-line still carried a thin warmth when she tested it, a band of elevated heat that did not match any fire or kiln vent.\n\nShe was copying what the city had already written.\n\n“Merrow!”\n\nThe shout came from upslope. Tiffani finished the last chalk pass and looked up.\n\nTwo men in Merro Glass vests picked their way through rubble, their hems gray with dust. Behind them walked a third man with a blue sash at his shoulder—RRO. The sash was dirty but tied precisely, the knot centered as if that could impose order on cracked street.\n\nTiffani resisted the urge to wipe her hands on her tunic. The chalk and dust were already part of her.\n\n“Master Merrow,” the taller guild man called again when he drew close enough to stop shouting. “You have a gift for being where no one can find you.”\n\n“Stones keep moving,” she said. “I keep following.”\n\nHe glanced at the chalk lines and the copper-smudged seams and made a face that tried for humor and failed. “Yes. About that.” He nodded toward the sash. “Inspector Harlen. He came to the guild hall asking for someone who can read breaks. We told him you would already be in the street doing this.”\n\nHarlen stepped forward. His eyes took in her open logbook, the chalk on her fingers, the line she had drawn from wall to pavement. His gaze paused on the copper glints along one crack, then moved away as if he did not want to be seen noticing it.\n\n“Master Merrow,” he said. “The RRO is assembling a committee for hazard survey and repair coordination. You were recommended. We would prefer your attention on that instead of independent mapping.”\n\nTiffani shut the logbook partway, thumb holding her place. “The city is already performing a survey,” she said. “Fractures show where the load is failing. I am recording the result.”\n\n“The difference,” Harlen said, “is that a coordinated effort sets priorities. We need lists, not personal notebooks. The Crown wants counts: compromised supports, households at risk, mains threatened. If you join formally, you will have assistants and access to full plans.”\n\n“And constraints,” Tiffani said. “Assignments. Approved routes.”\n\n“Guidelines,” Harlen corrected. “We must manage panic as much as stone. If every craftsperson follows their own urgency, we lose coherence. The RRO needs your precision in a framework.”\n\nShe looked past him to the nearest crack. It bent away from where the official street plan claimed the main support should run. It bent toward the spot where Jhace’s hands had been. The heat band there had not faded fully.\n\n“You will prioritize by district,” she said. “By tax value. By which voices reach the Council chambers without runners.”\n\nHarlen did not deny it. His jaw tightened, then he chose a calmer tone. “We will prioritize where failure risks the greatest loss. Load density. Population. Critical services. A collapse near the Crown mains—”\n\n“Kills different names,” Tiffani said. “Not fewer bodies elsewhere.”\n\nOne of the guild men shifted his weight. “Tiffani,” he said, dropping formality. “The workshop needs you too. Orders are stacked. People want replacement glazing, safe mirrors, anything that makes a room feel less broken. You could move your bench upslope for a few Tides. Let the RRO handle the cracks and come back when the shouting settles.”\n\nThere it was: the clean line, the profitable line, the line that required less argument.\n\nHer mind supplied Marisse without permission. Not as a ghost or a lesson, just a fact: a person crushed under glass that should have been rated higher, in a shop that should have been reinforced, after Tiffani had written **noise** in the margin and turned the page to keep schedule.\n\nShe kept her voice even.\n\n“I have watched one pattern until it killed someone,” she said. “I will not repeat that because it is simpler for the Crown.”\n\nHarlen’s expression cooled. “Refusing a formal role means you will have no influence when we set priorities,” he said. “You can speak from the street, but decisions will still be made in chambers.”\n\n“Then I will give you numbers from the street,” she said. “You want lists? I will send lists. Holds. Duration ranges. Estimated loads. Heat bands. Copper residue. You can compare my sheets to your minutes and decide which predicts the next collapse.”\n\nHe stared at her logbook, then back at her face. “You are making this harder than it needs to be.”\n\n“It became hard when the sky split,” she said. “Everything since is choosing where to stand.”\n\nHarlen let a short breath out through his nose. “Very well. The offer stands if you change your mind. Send your lists to the RRO hall. We will review them.”\n\nHe nodded to the guild men and turned away. They followed, one of them giving her a final look that carried workshop, pay, and the comfort of problems that ended at the edge of a bench.\n\nTiffani watched them go until their backs disappeared behind a leaning front.\n\nThen she reopened her logbook and turned to a clean page.\n\nShe wrote: **Post-Piercing Holds — source unknown**.\n\nUnder it she drew a narrow rectangle for the healer’s wall she had inspected earlier, marking the hot band where Jhace had touched and shading the cooler stone outside it. **Effective bearing width less than half original span.** **Stable now. Unsafe for long occupancy.**\n\nShe turned the page and began a table.\n\n**Location. Apparent load. Visible copper. Heat band. Reported hold duration. Outcome. Observed cost on subject.**\n\nThe last column stayed mostly blank. She had only one clear data point so far: his hands shaking, his forearms lit with vein-lines during use, his grip failing afterward on small objects. That was enough to justify more observation. It was not enough to predict how long he could keep doing it.\n\nShe wrote at the bottom: **Hypothesis: activation tied to immediate protective need. Noncritical conditions do not trigger. Cost accumulative. Data incomplete.**\n\nPella found her after a few spans, boots scraping over grit. Dust coated her lower legs up to the knee, and her curls had frizzed from heat and sweat. She carried a satchel that bulged with wrapped bread, stoppered jars, and a small ink bottle cushioned in cloth.\n\n“You were not at the workshop,” Pella said. “So I followed the noise. Also the runners saying ‘the glass woman with the book is marking walls again.’ Eat.”\n\nShe pushed bread into Tiffani’s chest with enough force that Tiffani had to trap it against her logbook.\n\nTiffani exhaled. “You are gentle today.”\n\n“I am consistent,” Pella said. “You forget to chew when you find a new problem. I am preventing structural failure.”\n\nTiffani opened the bread with her thumb and took a bite. It tasted of ash and salt. It also tasted of being steadied.\n\nPella leaned in to squint at the page. “That a new table?”\n\n“Yes,” Tiffani said. “For holds. For what the stone does afterward. For what he does afterward.”\n\n“The man with the glow,” Pella said.\n\n“The man with the tremor,” Tiffani corrected, and her voice softened despite herself. “He is pretending it is old injury. He is not good at pretending.”\n\nPella’s eyebrows lifted. “You got his name.”\n\n“He shouted it at a wall,” Tiffani said. “His friend shouted it louder. Saint Merro repeats anything that keeps people alive.”\n\nPella shifted her gaze toward the street, toward the patched wall and its propped poles. “So the guild wants you polishing rich panes, the RRO wants you behind a desk, and you want to walk broken streets counting how much that man is breaking himself.”\n\n“Yes,” Tiffani said. “That is accurate.”\n\nPella made a small sound that held irritation and acceptance. “Then you will need more ink,” she said, opening her satchel. “And you will need to sit once in a while. Sitting is permitted. I am granting permission.”\n\n“I do not require permission.”\n\n“You do,” Pella said, and pushed the ink bottle into her hand. “From me. I am a practical authority.”\n\nTiffani’s mouth pulled tight at the edges. It was the nearest she came to a smile without wasting attention.\n\nThey worked the street in a tight loop after that—walk, measure, mark, write. Three more sites where witnesses claimed copper shimmer had touched stone. At one, the residue had cooled to nothing but a faint discoloration. At another, a thin warmth still clung in a band that made Tiffani’s skin prickle when she held her palm near it. Each time, she sketched the fractures, traced probable load paths, and compared them to what standard construction demanded.\n\nEach time, the numbers returned the same message: the holds were narrow, inefficient, and only long enough.\n\nLate in Shaping she claimed a corner of a mostly intact shop whose owner had turned his counter into a writing surface. She weighted her pages with bits of broken stone and wrote patterns as statements, not comforts.\n\n**Confirmed holds only with people in direct harm path.**  \n**No confirmed holds on empty structures.**  \n**Copper intensity correlates with number of people cleared, not total mass.**  \n**Subject fine grip degraded post-use.**  \n\nShe circled **cost** three times, then stopped because repetition did not add information.\n\nWhen she lifted her head, her neck ached and the light outside the doorway had shifted. The street noise had changed from sharp panic to strained routine: people settling into the idea that this would not end in a single Tide. Under it all, the grid hum still sat at its new rough pitch.\n\nTiffani shut the logbook and pressed its cover against her sternum for a breath. Her chest rose and fell. The metallic tang in the air did not leave.\n\nWhen she stepped back into the street, her eyes went first to the healer’s wall. Someone had added poles and wedges where she had marked risk. The crack still held its new lean. No fresh dust spilled.\n\nBeyond it, a small knot of people had gathered near another run entrance, attention fixed on a pair of figures in the open.\n\nJhace Torrins stood there with Bram at his shoulder.\n\nHe looked worse in full light than he had when he was braced against the wall. The tremor in his hands showed even at this distance, fingers making constant small corrections that never settled. His shoulders carried the set of someone who had already pushed past what his body wanted and intended to do it again.\n\nHe reached down for something at his feet. He missed. He tried again and missed. On the third attempt his fingers caught the object, then jolted and let it fall. He straightened with his hands tucked behind his back, motion controlled and practiced.\n\nBram’s face turned toward him with a look Tiffani recognized without needing a name for it: concern that refused to become a command.\n\nTiffani flipped her logbook open and wrote one last line in the cost column.\n\n**Fine grip failure observed. Concealment behavior present.**\n\nShe shut the book, tucked it under her arm, and started toward him.\n\nIf the city insisted on building stories around a man who held stone with his bare hands, then someone needed to anchor those stories to marks and heat bands before they drove him into holds that would tear him apart.\n\nShe walked with measured steps, counting cracks as she went, and did not allow herself to look away from the tremor he kept hidden."
          ]
        },

    "f15a13c5-f22b-41f1-8e19-34b4e1066f3d": {
      "children": [
        "d8795b1a-9945-4b9b-bfa9-f4fd19f98ce6"
      ],
      "id": "f15a13c5-f22b-41f1-8e19-34b4e1066f3d",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": null,
          "role": "assistant"
        },
        "channel": "final",
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "Let’s lock Chapter 4 with that tiny lexicon tweak and a touch more “measurement talk” from Tiffani at the wall, without changing any events or beats.\n\n---\n\n# Chapter 4: After the Shudder  \n\nJhace woke to stone under his shoulders and a smell of boiled herbs.\n\nFor a breath he thought he was still in the street. Then the sounds sorted: not collapse, but low talk. Cloth rustling. Metal in basins. Somewhere a child crying, then hushed. No roar in the walls, only a roughened grid thrum under everything.\n\nHis hands hurt.\n\nHe tried to flex his fingers. Tremor ran from fingertips to elbow in a fine, constant shake. Pain followed, a dull burn along bone. Someone had wrapped rough cloth around his palms; the bandage edges scraped.\n\n“Stay down,” a voice said. Bram.\n\nJhace turned his head. He lay on a low pallet in a side room off a wider tunnel. The ceiling sat higher than an Underface run, stone mortared in regular blocks. Someone had cleared space and laid out more pallets in two rows. A triage nook, then. He recognized the top of a ladder shaft in the next room, the one near Bram’s corridor.\n\nBram sat on an overturned crate a pace away, elbows on his knees, face drawn. A bruise had started along one cheekbone, dark under dust. He held a clay cup in both hands.\n\n“How long,” Jhace said. His voice came out rough.\n\n“Since late Striking,” Bram answered. “We’re in Tide One now. Kindling’s already moving. You were out for most of that last stretch.”\n\nJhace pushed himself up onto an elbow. The room spun once and steadied. His stomach rolled; he ignored it.\n\n“Chloen,” he said.\n\n“Alive.” Bram’s mouth twitched. “Leg is… odd. She should have lost it. Healer says crushed bone, torn flesh. But when they cleaned it, some of the worst breaks were already knitting. Still bad. Not that bad. She’s under enough chew-root to sleep through a collapse.”\n\nJhace’s gaze slid past him. Chloen lay three pallets down. Her leg was splinted from hip to ankle, wrapped in clean cloth, stained through in a few places. Her chest rose and fell slow and even. Copper shimmer clung faintly at the edges of the bandages.\n\nJhace’s fingers jerked. Memory came in pieces: timber weight, heat in his hands, copper-gold sliding into bruised flesh for a flash.\n\nHe swallowed.\n\n“Do not do that,” Bram said quietly. “Do not put that on your list. She’s breathing because of you. Whatever else that was.”\n\nJhace looked down at his own hands. The cloth around them was discolored with dust and a faint copper stain. Under the bandages, his fingers twitched in small, useless patterns.\n\n“What do they say happened,” he asked.\n\n“Out there?” Bram jerked his chin toward the doorway. “Depends who you ask. Some say a falling building caught itself. Some say the Piercing tore the rules and Saint Merro’s stones are waking up angry. Some say there’s a Forgemaster with molten hands walking the streets.” His eyes went back to Jhace’s bandages. “Plenty say a grid rat in the Underface held a whole corner on his own.”\n\nJhace’s stomach tightened.\n\n“They’re asking for you,” Bram went on. “The ones who were under that wall. Runners keep sticking their heads in here. Asking if the man who held it is ready to go again.”\n\n“I held a span,” Jhace said. His throat tightened around the word. “It did not fall because the loads settled on another path. They want the wrong thing.”\n\n“Doesn’t change what they want,” Bram said. He set the cup on the floor. “City’s cracked. Council is arguing in the Crown. The guilds are yelling at each other about whose task this is. Meanwhile the stone fails where it pleases. We have work.”\n\nJhace swung his legs over the side of the pallet. His feet hit cold stone. Tremor raced up his arms in answer to the shift.\n\nBram reached to steady him. Jhace shook his head once. He pushed himself upright without help. The room lurched, then steadied.\n\n“Bad idea,” Bram muttered. “Very on-brand.”\n\n“Show me what failed,” Jhace said.\n\nBram held his gaze for a few breaths, then sighed.\n\n“Fine. But I walk beside you. If you fall, you don’t take a brace with you.”\n\nJhace said nothing. He bent to pull the bandages loose enough for his fingers to move. The cloth stuck in a few places; pain flared. The tremor did not care.\n\nThey stepped out into the main tunnel.\n\nThis level had become a passage more crowded than it had ever been. People moved in steady lines: runners with messages, guild workers with tools, healers with baskets. A smell of blood and hot metal hung under the usual damp stone. The grid’s hum had shifted; it ran harsher now, edges rough where pipes had taken damage.\n\nAs Jhace and Bram merged into the flow, heads turned.\n\n“That’s him,” someone murmured. “The one from the street.”\n\nJhace kept his eyes on the tunnel ahead. He let their words slide off.\n\n“Where first,” he asked.\n\n“Nearest failures we know about.” Bram pointed river-side. “Two marry points deformed. One small support crack we can’t spare full crews for yet. Figured we’d start there. Test the grid, test your legs.”\n\n“Test my hands,” Jhace said.\n\nBram’s mouth flattened.\n\nThey reached the first damaged junction after a short walk. The brace there had sheared partially away from the wall. Temporary wedges and a strap had been set to carry most of the load until a full crew could replace it. The main itself hummed steady; the bracket, not so much.\n\nJhace set his fingertips against the stone.\n\nThe vibration there told a clear story: stress transferred, not perfect but stable enough for now.\n\n“We move on,” he said.\n\nThe next problem sat deeper, in a lower run where the air grew hotter. A side support there had cracked along its base. Someone had braced it with a stack of loose stone, but the contact was poor; gaps showed.\n\n“No one under this line?” Jhace asked.\n\n“Not if they listened,” Bram said. “Street above already cleared.”\n\nJhace studied the crack. Not immediate collapse. Not harmless either. A failure here in the next Tide would drop a section of tunnel and weaken the wall of the building over it.\n\nIt would be days before the guild could spare enough workers.\n\nHe put his palm on the broken base.\n\nStone met skin. Cool under the surface, warmer where it held load. He could feel the fracture: a thin gap, rough edges ready to spread. Tremor in his hand muddied the read.\n\nHe closed his eyes.\n\nHold, he told whatever this new thing was. Hold here. Small span. No one is under it, but they will be.\n\nNothing changed.\n\nNo heat surge. No copper-gold. Only the familiar push of load against his hand and the tremor in his own fingers.\n\nHe pressed harder. Pain flared in his palm, then his wrist. The crack did not answer.\n\nBram watched from a pace away.\n\n“Anything?” he asked.\n\n“No,” Jhace said.\n\n“Good,” Bram said. “Because if you were about to tell me you can now fix every hairline scratch in this city with your glow, I was going to go lie down in traffic.”\n\nJhace pulled his hand back. His skin tingled from the effort, but the stone stayed unchanged.\n\n“Not on demand,” he said.\n\n“So we learn something,” Bram said. “Whatever that was, it’s not a new wrench you can hang on your belt. Needs more than a bad seam.”\n\nMore than a bad seam. He thought of Chloen’s leg under the beam. The faces under the building corner. His own head and chest had been full of one thing then: do not let this kill them.\n\nHe flexed his fingers. Tremor answered, unchanged.\n\n“Mark this for priority reinforcement,” he said. “We do it by hand.”\n\nThey climbed back toward the surface.\n\nThe next failure found them before they reached open air.\n\nA runner nearly collided with them at a junction. A boy, sweat-slick, chest heaving.\n\n“You Jhace Torrins?” the boy gasped. “The holder?”\n\nJhace’s shoulders tightened.\n\n“Yes,” Bram said, before Jhace could decide whether to argue with the word. “What’s failing?”\n\n“Small shop front near the Merro kiln corridor,” the boy said. “Wall cracked. Healers set up inside, working triage. Master said to get the holder, or we lose half the wounded when it goes.”\n\nJhace didn’t look at Bram. He just started moving.\n\nThe street near the kilns was a mess.\n\nOne whole row of smaller buildings had taken impact from falling debris. Most still stood, but faces were fractured, bits of stone missing, wooden flanges splintered. Someone had chosen a shop with only one outer wall to convert into a healer’s space. The wounded lay on tables and pallets inside. The wall that separated them from the street now showed a visible crack from lintel to ground.\n\nPeople crowded the doorway. Some tried to brace the wall with poles and stacked debris. The fracture deepened a grain at a time as the crowd jostled.\n\n“Back,” Jhace said.\n\nNo one moved at first. His voice came out raw, not loud.\n\nBram stepped up beside him.\n\n“You heard him,” Bram said. “Back. Give stone room to breathe.”\n\nThe crowd parted in jerks and mutters. Jhace stepped to the crack.\n\nUp close, it looked worse. Fine dust leaked from it in a slow stream. The load path for the roof had shifted onto two narrow points instead of a full line. The shop’s inner ceiling beams rested on that.\n\nSeveral wounded lay directly under them.\n\nJhace put his palms flat on the wall at the crack’s middle. Cold from outside, heat from bodies inside, pressure from above.\n\nHold, he thought. They die if you do not hold.\n\nHeat rose under his hands. Not as sharp as under the beam or the street support, but fast enough. Copper-gold bloomed across his skin and slid into the stone, thin lines chasing the fracture up and down. They reached the lintel and the floor, then flowed sideways into the adjoining blocks.\n\nHe gritted his teeth. His arms went rigid.\n\n“Clear under the center span,” Bram shouted. “Move the pallets outward. Go!”\n\nVoices answered in fragments. He heard scrambling inside. Cloth dragged. Someone groaned in pain as they were shifted.\n\nThe wall pressed against his hands, trying to move. The crack wanted to widen. He could feel every point that carried too much now: bearing reduced to those two narrow contacts instead of the full length. His pulse beat inside his fingertips.\n\nHis own shake intensified. The copper-gold in his forearms lit vein-lines down to his elbows. Every heartbeat drove a fresh spike of pain along them.\n\n“Almost,” Bram called. “Hold.”\n\nJhace held.\n\nBreaths blurred. The glow spread as far as it could find stress. Then it stopped. It did not mend. It only kept the worst from moving.\n\n“Clear,” Bram shouted at last. “Everyone out from center.”\n\nJhace counted three heartbeats more, then let go.\n\nThe copper-gold cut off. The wall shifted a small amount, then caught on a new angle, the crack now filled with fine stone dust and glass-bright residue. Some loose chips fell and bounced off the cleared floor. The ceiling beams groaned but stayed where they were.\n\nJhace’s hands dropped to his sides. His fingers clawed without his consent, opening and closing in jittering motions.\n\n“Out,” Bram told the crowd. “No one rests under that span. You—” he pointed at a pair of workers “—get poles and a proper brace on it before next Pouring.”\n\nJhace stepped back until his shoulders hit the opposite wall. The world had gone slightly out of focus at the edges. Sweat dripped down his back. His hands shook hard enough that his nails clicked against his own skin.\n\n“Your arms,” Bram said, low. “Veins went bright all the way up. Then nothing. You still with me?”\n\n“For now,” Jhace said. His tongue felt thick.\n\nHe looked down. Faint copper-gold lines still traced his forearms, pulsing in time with the tremor. They faded slowly, but the shake did not.\n\n“Well,” Bram said. “We learned the other half.”\n\n“What half,” Jhace asked.\n\n“Whatever this is,” Bram said, “it answers when people are about to get crushed. It does not answer when you want to fix a crack for later. And every time it listens, it takes more out of you.”\n\nJhace didn’t answer. He stared at his hands until the copper traces vanished, leaving only red skin and the unsteady twitch.\n\nSomeone cleared their throat to his left.\n\nThe woman from the street stood there. The one with glass dust in her hair and the logbook clutched to her chest. She had cleaned up some since he’d seen her last; the dust now lined her clothes instead of her face. Her eyes were sharper than before.\n\n“Jhace Torrins,” she said.\n\nHe narrowed his eyes.\n\n“You know my name.”\n\n“You shouted it at the wall yesterday,” she said. “Bram yelled it three times. And half the runners are using it now.” She looked at his hands. “May I see where you touched?”\n\n“No,” he said.\n\nHer brows lifted a fraction.\n\n“I need to understand what changed in the stone,” she said. “People are setting up triage in buildings you’ve… supported. If I know where it’s strongest, I can tell them where not to lie down.”\n\nJhace exhaled through his teeth. That, at least, was an argument he could not throw aside.\n\nHe turned back to the cracked wall and set one shaking finger against the point where the glow had felt thickest. The motion was clumsy. His fingertip missed twice before finding the line.\n\n“Here,” he said. “And here.” He traced the path down and across as best he could.\n\nShe stepped in closer, careful not to touch him. Her gaze followed his hand, then left it, measuring the stone on its own.\n\n“The temperature is wrong,” she murmured. “Hot along that line, cooler outside it. The stress reads different too.” She pressed her palm to the wall, eyes half closed. “Load is still carried, but the bearing area has shrunk. It shouldn’t be standing. But it is.”\n\n“It is standing for now,” Jhace said. “Do not trust it for more than that.”\n\n“I don’t trust anything for more than that,” she said. “But I prefer numbers to stories. Stories are already taking root about you.”\n\n“Stories are useless,” Jhace said. “Either the brace holds or it doesn’t.”\n\n“And if it only holds because you touch it?” she asked.\n\nHe had no answer.\n\nShe looked down at his hands again. The tremor had not eased. He tried to still his fingers; they shook anyway.\n\n“Is that new?” she asked.\n\n“Old injury,” he said. The lie came fast, worn from too much practice over the Tide. “Tools do that.”\n\n“Mm,” she said. She did not sound convinced. “If you keep lying badly, you’re going to make my work harder.”\n\nBefore he could reply, someone called her name from up the street. She glanced toward the sound, then back at him.\n\n“I’m Tiffani Merrow,” she said. “Glasswork and structural optics.” She nodded toward the wall. “I intend to make sure people don’t sleep where your grips will fail first. That requires data.”\n\n“I have routes,” Jhace said. “I don’t have data.”\n\n“You have both,” she said. “You just don’t write all of it down yet.”\n\nShe stepped away, already scanning the next stack of damaged fronts, already turning the scene into columns in her head. Bram watched her go with a low whistle.\n\n“She’s going to be trouble,” he said.\n\nJhace opened his mouth to tell him he already had enough trouble and dropped the words.\n\nA bent nail lay near his boot. He bent to pick it up.\n\nHis fingers missed it twice. On the third try he managed to trap it between thumb and forefinger, but the tremor jerked it free. It clinked back to stone.\n\nHe stared at it.\n\nBram’s gaze slid toward his hands.\n\nJhace straightened slowly. He put his hands behind his back, hiding the twitch.\n\n“I’m fine,” he said.\n\nBram did not answer.\n\nAcross the street, Tiffani watched them. Her eyes dropped once to Jhace’s hidden hands, then back to his face. She said nothing either.\n\nThe grid hummed at its new pitch under their feet. The wall held. Jhace’s grip did not."
          ]
        },

    "f6211277-2324-4149-aa58-f4870bedd2f5": {
      "children": [
        "409a1dc3-1feb-40a5-86f0-5e5bebc4c54d"
      ],
      "id": "f6211277-2324-4149-aa58-f4870bedd2f5",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": null,
          "role": "user"
        },
        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "# N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS - CHAPTER GRID (12-CHAPTER STRUCTURE)\n**REVISED EDITION - MANDATORY REFERENCE FOR ALL PROSE GENERATION**\n\n---\n\n## CRITICAL NOTICE\n\n**THIS GRID MUST BE CONSULTED BEFORE:**\n- Writing any new prose\n- Revising any existing prose\n- Making structural decisions\n- Adjusting beat assignments\n- Moving content between chapters\n\n**ANY DEVIATION FROM THIS GRID REQUIRES EXPLICIT AUTHORIZATION AND GRID UPDATE.**\n\n---\n\n## NOVELLA PARAMETERS\n\n- **Novella ID:** N01\n- **Title:** The Grip That Holds\n- **Turning:** 1 - GLASS (Fragile formation, first awakening)\n- **Acrostic Letter:** G (first novella of GLASS)\n- **Structure:** 12 chapters + 2 interludes + 1 epilogue = 15 segments\n- **Word Count Target:** 18,000-21,000 words total\n- **Chapter Target Range:** 1,200-2,200 words (variance permitted for beat requirements)\n- **Timeline Covered:** Tide 0 (Piercing) through Tide 20 (~20 days post-Piercing)\n\n---\n\n## ACT STRUCTURE\n\n### **ACT I: THE CUT (Chapters 1-4)**\n- **Function:** Crisis establishes characters, introduces Soulpulse, first contact\n- **Word Count:** ~9,170 words (45% of novella)\n- **Chapters:** 4\n- **Emotional Arc:** Low → Low → High → Medium\n\n### **ACT II: THE PATTERN (Chapters 5-9)**\n- **Function:** Partnership forms, patterns emerge, institutional pressure builds\n- **Word Count:** ~8,900 words (43% of novella)\n- **Chapters:** 5 (includes Interlude I after Ch 7)\n- **Emotional Arc:** Medium → Medium → Low → Medium → Medium\n\n### **ACT III: THE GRIP (Chapters 10-12)**\n- **Function:** Crisis tests bond, commitment made, foundation locked\n- **Word Count:** ~5,200 words (25% of novella)\n- **Chapters:** 3 (includes Interlude II after Ch 10)\n- **Emotional Arc:** High → High → Medium\n\n---\n\n## CHAPTER GRID\n\n### **CHAPTER 1: \"Something Wrong\"**\n- **POV:** Jhace (tight third)\n- **Tide:** 0, Kindling (morning of Piercing)\n- **Setting:** Underface beneath Saint Merro, heat-grid inspection run\n- **Beat Method:** BEAT_01 (Observation → Pattern Recognition)\n- **Emotional Intensity:** Low\n- **Word Count:** 1,240 words\n- **Status:** 🔒 LOCKED\n\n**Summary:**\nJhace inspects heat-grid junctions in his ritualistic pre-Piercing routine. He detects an \"off-note\" in the grid hum—pressure deviation signaling potential failure. Bram teases him about working alone and obsessive checking patterns. Ground tremor hits. Jhace feels atmospheric change (copper tang, pressure shift). Chapter establishes his trauma-driven vigilance and introduces Tiffani through guild gossip as \"the glassworker with impossible standards.\"\n\n**Key Beats:**\n- Opens with wrong hum detection (immediate sensory hook)\n- Establishes Underface setting and Jhace's expertise\n- Bram introduces caretaker/control theme\n- First tremor (foreshadowing)\n- Ends with Jhace at street level, sensing wrongness\n\n**Soulpulse:** Not yet accessible (pre-Piercing)\n\n**Relationship:** Jhace aware of Tiffani by reputation only; judges her as rigid perfectionist\n\n**Stage Progression:** N/A (Stage 0)\n\n---\n\n### **CHAPTER 2: \"Glass That Listens\"**\n- **POV:** Tiffani (tight third)\n- **Tide:** 0, Pouring (mid-morning of Piercing)\n- **Setting:** Observatory platform at Saint Merro's rim\n- **Beat Method:** BEAT_01 (Observation → Pattern Recognition)\n- **Emotional Intensity:** Low\n- **Word Count:** 1,280 words\n- **Status:** 🔒 LOCKED\n\n**Summary:**\nTiffani measures atmospheric distortion from her observatory, detecting the incoming Piercing object through theodolite readings. She has been awake for days, driven by perfectionism rooted in Marisse's death. Pella brings food and scolds her for not resting. Measurements show descent trajectory—object is not passing over, it's coming down. First tremor hits. White line appears in sky. Tiffani and Pella evacuate as roar begins.\n\n**Key Beats:**\n- Mirrors Ch 1 structure (parallel observation)\n- Establishes Tiffani's precision methodology\n- Pella introduces care/support dynamic\n- Marisse trauma implied through vigilance pattern\n- Atmospheric distortion measurements (first scientific data)\n- Exit as sky splits\n\n**Soulpulse:** Not yet accessible (pre-Piercing)\n\n**Relationship:** Tiffani aware of Jhace by reputation only; judges him as overextending ego-driven builder\n\n**Stage Progression:** N/A (Stage 0)\n\n---\n\n### **CHAPTER 3: \"The Sky Breaks\"**\n- **POV:** Jhace (tight third)\n- **Tide:** 0, Striking (Piercing event)\n- **Setting:** Underface tunnel, then street level at building support\n- **Beat Method:** BEAT_03 (Crisis Front-Loaded → Rolling Complications → Partial Resolution)\n- **Emotional Intensity:** High\n- **Word Count:** 2,200 words\n- **Status:** 🔒 LOCKED\n\n**Summary:**\nPiercing hits. Underface collapses. Chloen pinned under beam. Jhace's first Soulpulse use (desperate protective need + contact = activation). Beam lightens enough to free her; side-effect healing visible (copper-gold on bruised flesh, breaks knitting). Cost: tremor, vein-lines, pain. They escape to surface. Second Soulpulse use on failing building support (people in doorway). Jhace holds long enough for evacuation. Greater cost: worse tremor, vision narrowing, collapse. Wakes to Tiffani observing him with logbook, asking \"What did you do?\"\n\n**Key Beats:**\n- Opens mid-catastrophe (wall moves before Jhace does)\n- Chloen trapped, Jhace's trauma activated (23 names callback)\n- First Soulpulse: contact + desperate need = copper-gold glow\n- Side-effect healing shown but not explained\n- Surface escape, white line visible overhead\n- Second Soulpulse on building support\n- Cost escalation: tremor → vein-lines → collapse\n- J/T meeting: analytical demand vs. exhausted answer\n\n**Soulpulse:** 2 uses (beam hold, building support)\n- Requirements confirmed: skin contact + desperate protective emotion\n- Visuals: copper-gold glow, spreads into material, traces stress lines\n- Cost: heat in palms during, tremor/vein-lines/pain/exhaustion after\n\n**Relationship:** First meeting through competence recognition (she measures, he builds)\n\n**Stage Progression:** 0 → 1 onset (tremor baseline establishing)\n\n---\n\n### **CHAPTER 4: \"After the Shudder\"**\n- **POV:** Jhace (tight third)\n- **Tide:** 1, Kindling (morning after Piercing)\n- **Setting:** Triage chamber, Underface tunnels, kiln street\n- **Beat Method:** BEAT_02 (Trial → Failure → Compounding → Success/Cost)\n- **Emotional Intensity:** Medium\n- **Word Count:** 2,350 words\n- **Status:** 🔒 LOCKED\n\n**Summary:**\nJhace wakes with persistent tremor and pain. Bram reports Chloen's leg healing unexpectedly. Jhace tests power on cracked support without immediate threat—FAILS (no activation). Runner brings emergency: healer's triage wall failing with wounded inside. Immediate threat present—power WORKS but cost increases (vein-lines to elbows, worse tremor). Tiffani observes, asks to see touch points for data collection. Jhace tries to pick up nail—fails three times, tremor jerks it free. Concealment behavior (hands behind back). Tiffani sees everything, documents cost in logbook.\n\n**Key Beats:**\n- Wakes to baseline tremor (Stage 1 established)\n- Chloen's healing confirmed (side-effect established)\n- Power test on non-critical crack: FAILS (conditional activation shown)\n- Emergency at triage wall: SUCCEEDS (immediate threat required)\n- Cost progression: worse tremor, can't grip nail (functional loss)\n- Tiffani introduction proper: analytical, demands data\n- J/T dynamic: reluctant compliance because her logic is sound\n- Exit: \"The wall held. Jhace's grip did not.\"\n\n**Soulpulse:** 1 successful use (triage wall), 1 failed attempt (cracked support)\n- Conditional activation confirmed: requires immediate protective need\n- Cost accumulation shown: Stage 1 baseline tremor, fine motor compromised\n\n**Relationship:** Tiffani decides to engage deliberately (prevent his destruction through measurement)\n\n**Stage Progression:** Stage 1 baseline established, functional loss beginning\n\n---\n\n### **CHAPTER 5: \"Lines That Don't Lie\"**\n- **POV:** Tiffani (tight third)\n- **Tide:** 2-3 (days after Piercing)\n- **Setting:** Kiln corridor streets, various hold sites\n- **Beat Method:** BEAT_04 (Investigation → Survey → Pattern Recognition → Decision)\n- **Emotional Intensity:** Medium\n- **Word Count:** 2,100 words\n- **Status:** 🔒 LOCKED\n\n**Summary:**\nTiffani systematically maps post-Piercing damage, measuring copper residue and heat bands from Jhace's holds. RRO Inspector Harlen offers her formal committee position—she REFUSES, prioritizing street-level truth over institutional control. Guild offers safe workshop retreat—she REFUSES, citing Marisse's death as reason not to optimize for comfort. She documents hold patterns: only activates with people in direct danger, copper intensity correlates with lives at risk not mass, effective bearing zones dangerously narrow. Pella brings food and ink, enables her work. Tiffani observes Jhace from distance: sees tremor, sees three failed attempts to pick up object, sees concealment behavior. Decides to engage directly to prevent his destruction.\n\n**Key Beats:**\n- Opens with systematic mapping (investigation methodology)\n- RRO pressure: refuses institutional co-option\n- Guild pressure: refuses profitable retreat\n- Marisse callback: guilt drives street-level choice\n- Pattern documentation: conditional activation, cost progression\n- Pella support: practical care without coddling\n- Observes Jhace's decline from distance\n- Decision: engage to anchor stories with data before they kill him\n\n**Soulpulse:** None (observation chapter)\n\n**Relationship:** Tiffani chooses Jhace as problem to solve, not hero to follow\n\n**Stage Progression:** External observation of Stage 1 effects\n\n---\n\n### **CHAPTER 6: \"Weight That Won't Yield\"**\n- **POV:** Jhace (tight third)\n- **Tide:** 3-4\n- **Setting:** Major structural failure at Saint Merro junction\n- **Beat Method:** BEAT_03 (Crisis Front-Loaded → Sustained Pressure → Narrow Success)\n- **Emotional Intensity:** Medium-High\n- **Target Word Count:** ~1,800 words\n- **Status:** TO BE WRITTEN\n\n**Summary:**\nMulti-story arch threatening collapse. Jhace attempts traditional propping—insufficient. Tiffani arrives with survey data, sketches stress lines on stone (showing him geometry of what he feels). They coordinate without planning: she marks three critical points, he focuses Soulpulse on those exact locations. First true choreographed hold. Success but greater cost: tremor worsens, vein-lines persist longer. Tiffani critiques his \"inefficient\" force application—her precision could reduce his cost by 30%. Jhace's reaction: (1) she's critiquing me, (2) she's right, (3) she sees my inefficiency and wants to fix it. Partnership recognition through competence.\n\n**Key Beats:**\n- Crisis opening: arch failing, people still inside\n- Jhace's traditional methods insufficient\n- Tiffani arrives with data/geometry\n- First coordinated Soulpulse (she marks, he powers)\n- Success with visible cost increase\n- Her critique of his technique (care disguised as analysis)\n- His recognition: she sees him, not his heroism\n- Exit: they've choreographed perfectly without planning\n\n**Soulpulse:** 1 coordinated use (arch stabilization)\n- Demonstrates improved targeting reduces cost (her geometry + his power)\n- Still significant cost: tremor persistent, vein-lines linger\n\n**Relationship:** First true partnership action—coordination through shared competence\n\n**Stage Progression:** Stage 1 progressing, cost accumulation visible\n\n---\n\n### **CHAPTER 7: \"Quiet Calculations\"**\n- **POV:** Tiffani (tight third)\n- **Tide:** 5-6\n- **Setting:** Tiffani's workshop/planning space\n- **Beat Method:** BEAT_20 (Rest → Planning → Preparation)\n- **Emotional Intensity:** Low\n- **Target Word Count:** ~1,500 words\n- **Status:** TO BE WRITTEN\n\n**Summary:**\nTiffani compiles data from multiple hold sites, building predictive model for failure patterns. She discovers Jhace's cost curve is exponential not linear—he's approaching breaking point faster than he realizes. Pella asks why she cares so much about \"the man with the glow.\" Tiffani's answer: precision requires complete data, and he's destroying the most interesting variable. But underneath: she's starting to see him as person, not just phenomenon. She calculates how to approach him with data he'll actually hear (not orders, not concern, but operational logic). Ends with her walking toward his forge with documented proof he needs to see.\n\n**Key Beats:**\n- Data compilation and pattern analysis\n- Discovery: cost curve is exponential (crisis point approaching)\n- Pella questions her investment\n- Tiffani's rationalization vs. actual feeling\n- Strategic planning: how to make him listen\n- Decision to approach with evidence\n- Exit: walking toward forge with logbook\n\n**Soulpulse:** None (analysis chapter)\n\n**Relationship:** Tiffani recognizing care underneath her analytical framework\n\n**Stage Progression:** External calculation of his trajectory toward Stage 2\n\n---\n\n### **INTERLUDE I: \"Corlexi - System vs Chaos\"**\n- **POV:** Corlexi (Form god-vessel, mortal name unknown to self)\n- **Tide:** ~6-7\n- **Setting:** Crown administrative chambers, multiple observation points\n- **Type:** Build-Up\n- **Word Count Target:** 600-900 words\n- **Status:** TO BE WRITTEN\n\n**Summary:**\nTwo-segment structure. First segment: Corlexi reviews stabilization reports, sees Order taking shape through RRO formation. She feels satisfaction at frameworks solidifying but also unease—something escapes her control. The smith and glassworker refuse to be contained, building third path outside her geometry. Second segment: She experiences her Principle (Form) as compulsion to impose structure, feels nascent god-awareness stirring (vast power constrained by single lens). She makes note: must address the anomaly in Saint Merro. Closing: Auditor observes Corlexi's awakening, notes Form's limitation—cannot tolerate things outside patterns.\n\n**Key Elements:**\n- Sensory overwhelm technique (god-vessel perception)\n- Principle bias manifestation (everything seen through Form lens)\n- Innocent frame maintained (no recognition of divinity)\n- Jhace/Tiffani recognized as structural threat\n- Auditor frame at open/close\n\n**Purpose:** Establish divine forces moving, show limitation of single-Principle perspective, foreshadow institutional pressure in Act III\n\n---\n\n### **CHAPTER 8: \"Proof and Resistance\"**\n- **POV:** Jhace (tight third)\n- **Tide:** 7-8\n- **Setting:** Jhace's forge, then joint inspection of hold sites\n- **Beat Method:** BEAT_12 (Relationship Shift → Resistance → Acceptance)\n- **Emotional Intensity:** Medium\n- **Target Word Count:** ~1,600 words\n- **Status:** TO BE WRITTEN\n\n**Summary:**\nTiffani arrives at forge with compiled data, shows Jhace his cost curve projection. He resists—doesn't want to see himself as failing, doesn't trust numbers over experience. She challenges: \"You trust stone to tell truth. These marks are stone's truth about what you're doing to yourself.\" Tension: his defensive walls vs. her refusal to let him self-destruct through ignorance. She proposes operational change: she identifies critical points, he applies minimal force, they test if efficiency reduces cost. He agrees grudgingly—not because he believes her, but because her logic is sound even if he hates it. They test method on controlled scenario. It works. Cost is measurably lower. His recognition: accepting her help isn't weakness, it's competence.\n\n**Key Beats:**\n- Tiffani presents evidence (his cost curve)\n- Jhace's defensive resistance (control/autonomy threatened)\n- Her cutting through: stone doesn't lie, neither do these marks\n- Tension: care vs. pride\n- Proposal: test efficiency method\n- Controlled test succeeds\n- His grudging acceptance: she makes him better\n- Exit: agreement to coordinate on next real crisis\n\n**Soulpulse:** 1 controlled test (reduced cost confirmed with her targeting)\n\n**Relationship:** Resistance → acceptance that partnership improves both\n\n**Stage Progression:** Stage 1 stable, cost reduction possible with coordination\n\n---\n\n### **CHAPTER 9: \"Ash and Claims\"**\n- **POV:** Tiffani (tight third)\n- **Tide:** 10-12\n- **Setting:** Glass Quarter refugee camp, Crown administrative contact\n- **Beat Method:** BEAT_16 (Investigation → Discovery → Complication)\n- **Emotional Intensity:** Medium\n- **Target Word Count:** ~1,700 words\n- **Status:** TO BE WRITTEN\n\n**Summary:**\nCorvin (Mason faction) and Tellis (Water Guild) arrive at Glass Quarter offering \"help\" in exchange for political alignment. Tiffani calculates: resources needed, cost is autonomy. Logic says accept. But she checks variable: Jhace will see this as betrayal, and his trust affects coordination. Deeper: she wants his trust (not logical, but real). She pulls him aside, presents logic without asking permission. He asks: \"What do you want?\" She answers: keep people alive without becoming Corvin's tool (contradictory goals in his framework). Jhace: \"Then don't join him. I won't be vulnerable alone.\" They publicly refuse Corvin. Political consequence: now enemies of powerful faction. Personal consequence: they chose each other over safety. First explicit choice of partnership over pragmatism.\n\n**Key Beats:**\n- Corvin/Tellis offer (resources for control)\n- Tiffani's calculation (logic vs. feeling)\n- She consults Jhace (first time asking partner input)\n- His question: what do YOU want?\n- Her honest answer: contradictory desires\n- His commitment: vulnerable together\n- Public refusal of institutional power\n- Exit: political enemies made, personal bond strengthened\n\n**Soulpulse:** None (political chapter)\n\n**Relationship:** First explicit choice of each other over safety/pragmatism\n\n**Stage Progression:** Stable Stage 1 with coordinated cost management\n\n---\n\n### **CHAPTER 10: \"Near Collapse\"**\n- **POV:** Both (alternating perspective within chapter)\n- **Tide:** 14-15\n- **Setting:** Major structural crisis at Saint Merro main support\n- **Beat Method:** BEAT_03 (External Threat → Narrow Escape)\n- **Emotional Intensity:** High\n- **Target Word Count:** ~1,800 words\n- **Status:** TO BE WRITTEN\n\n**Summary:**\nCritical main support failing during high-traffic period. Hundreds at risk. Jhace and Tiffani coordinate under pressure: she calculates load paths in real-time, he applies force to her exact specifications. They move as single unit, no wasted words, pure operational sync. Success—but Jhace pushes past safe limits to ensure no one dies. Cost: approaches Stage 2 threshold (tremor violent, vein-lines permanent at baseline, brief moment of sensory overload). Tiffani physically restrains him from continuing, makes decision FOR him: \"You're done. I don't care if you hate me for it.\" Saves him by overriding his control. Afterward, his rage at her presumption vs. her cold certainty she was right. Tension peaks: control vs. care, autonomy vs. survival.\n\n**Key Beats:**\n- Crisis: hundreds at risk, main support failing\n- Perfect coordination under pressure\n- Jhace pushes past limits (caretaker compulsion)\n- Tiffani's override: physical intervention\n- Success but near-Stage 2 cost\n- His rage: she took his choice\n- Her certainty: he would have killed himself\n- Relationship stress: care as control\n- Exit: silent fury and mutual recognition they need each other\n\n**Soulpulse:** 1 major use (near-Stage 2 threshold)\n- Coordinated efficiency maintains, but scale drives cost\n- Brief sensory overload (Stage 2 warning sign)\n- Vein-lines now faintly visible at rest\n\n**Relationship:** Conflict through care—she saves him by controlling him, he hates needing it\n\n**Stage Progression:** Stage 1 → approaching Stage 2 threshold (warning signs present)\n\n---\n\n### **INTERLUDE II: \"Mobel - Purity Rising\"**\n- **POV:** Mobel (Purity god-vessel, mortal name unknown to self)\n- **Tide:** ~15-16\n- **Setting:** Entry-side (opposite hemisphere from Saint Merro)\n- **Type:** Build-Up\n- **Word Count Target:** 600-900 words\n- **Status:** TO BE WRITTEN\n\n**Summary:**\nTwo-segment structure. First segment: Mobel leads cleansing ritual where early resonants prove themselves through enduring pain without using Soulpulse. She feels power of her Principle—clarity, judgment, separation of righteous from corrupt. Second segment: Moment of god-awareness—she senses Form-aligned power across planet (Corlexi stirring). Recognition: there are others like her, alliance or war inevitable. Principle limitation shown: sees only absolutes, cannot tolerate ambiguity. Auditor observes: Purity will clash with Form's institutional approach, both will target Jhace/Tiffani's third path.\n\n**Key Elements:**\n- Sensory overwhelm (pain as purification)\n- Purity Principle bias (absolute judgment)\n- First inter-god awareness\n- Innocent frame (no recognition of divinity)\n- Foreshadows ideological warfare\n- Auditor frame\n\n**Purpose:** Establish second divine force, show Principles in conflict, set up multi-front pressure on protagonists\n\n---\n\n### **CHAPTER 11: \"Holding Together\"**\n- **POV:** Both (intimate alternating perspective)\n- **Tide:** 17-18\n- **Setting:** Quiet space after crisis (Jhace's forge or Tiffani's workshop)\n- **Beat Method:** BEAT_17 (Intimacy → Vulnerability → Connection)\n- **Emotional Intensity:** High\n- **Target Word Count:** ~1,900 words\n- **Status:** TO BE WRITTEN\n\n**Summary:**\nPost-Ch10 processing. Jhace's rage has cooled to exhaustion. Tiffani's certainty has cracked to fear—she nearly watched him die. Neither apologizes (both were right, both were wrong). Instead: vulnerability exchange. Jhace tells her about the building collapse that killed his parents, the 23 names he carries, why he can't choose who to save (it's choosing who dies). Tiffani tells him about Marisse, the miscalculation, why precision is penance not preference. Recognition: they're both trying to atone for deaths they couldn't prevent. She says: \"I need you to survive this.\" He says: \"I need you to keep stopping me.\" Not love confession—operational truth. They are load-bearing for each other. Moment of physical intimacy (forehead touch, hand hold—first deliberate contact not in crisis). Commitment without words.\n\n**Key Beats:**\n- Post-crisis exhaustion (defenses down)\n- Trauma exchange: his 23 names, her Marisse\n- Recognition: both atoning for unprevented deaths\n- Mutual need acknowledged (operational, not romantic)\n- Physical intimacy: first non-crisis contact\n- Commitment: she keeps him honest, he trusts her override\n- Exit: understanding they can't survive apart\n\n**Soulpulse:** None (recovery/intimacy chapter)\n\n**Relationship:** Trauma-bonding → mutual recognition of load-bearing function\n\n**Stage Progression:** Stage 1 recovering with rest, but baseline effects permanent\n\n---\n\n### **CHAPTER 12: \"Choosing the Weight\"**\n- **POV:** Both (balanced perspective)\n- **Tide:** 19-20\n- **Setting:** Rebuilt overlook above Saint Merro, forge wall\n- **Beat Method:** BEAT_21 (Rest → Reflection → Renewed Purpose)\n- **Emotional Intensity:** Medium\n- **Target Word Count:** ~1,600 words\n- **Status:** TO BE WRITTEN\n\n**Summary:**\nWeeks after immediate crisis. Jhace and Tiffani walk their district at Quench, seeing uneven results: some structures beautifully reinforced, others held by temporary measures, some families back in homes, others permanently displaced. Every scar holds memory. They discuss larger currents: registries spreading, clinics multiplying, RRO expanding, black-market training proliferating. They know storm is forming beyond their neighborhood. What they can control: what they hold. At forge, they write three priorities on wall: (1) Truth over comfort, (2) People over institutions, (3) Keep each other honest about why we act. Vow without ceremony. Community members add their marks beside it—informal pact, local mandate. Not oath to Crown or RRO, but commitment to third path. Jhace's hand shakes as he writes; Tiffani laces their fingers, lets his tremor move through both. Physical reminder: cost and commitment shared. Exit: they face uncertain future together, foundation locked.\n\n**Key Beats:**\n- Walk through rebuilt district (uneven success)\n- Recognition of larger forces gathering\n- Decision: focus on what they can hold\n- Three priorities written on forge wall\n- Community witnesses/joins commitment\n- Shared physical moment (tremor through linked hands)\n- Acceptance: this is who they are together\n- Exit: foundation locked, future uncertain but faced together\n\n**Soulpulse:** None (commitment/resolution chapter)\n\n**Relationship:** Partnership formalized through shared principles (not romance, but deeper)\n\n**Stage Progression:** Stage 1 baseline accepted as new permanent state\n\n---\n\n### **EPILOGUE: \"Auditor Observation\"**\n- **POV:** Auditor (meta-perspective outside time)\n- **Setting:** Auditor's observation point (undefined location)\n- **Word Count Target:** 800-1,000 words\n- **Status:** TO BE WRITTEN\n\n**Summary:**\nAuditor catalogs N01 outcomes: two mortals built unprecedented partnership balancing Form and Wholeness without ideology, created \"third path\" threatening all factions, refused institutional offers, committed to independence, wrote three priorities and community witnessed. Assessment of characters: Jhace unpredictable because care is absolute, Tiffani unpredictable because precision is absolute, both useful because they might survive what's coming. Interest in their bond: they love functionally (load-bearing systems), not romantically. Remove one, other fails. This is useful—when gods move and alliances fracture, bond will save or destroy them. Either way, worth observing. Decision: flag them as anomaly, predict system will move to crush or co-opt. Notes that Turning 1's hope phase shading toward devastation, their bond potential fulcrum or liability. If love makes people break rules, it reshapes wars—or ruins plans. Sets stage for N02 escalation.\n\n**Key Elements:**\n- Meta-perspective assessment\n- Character analysis (unpredictability as threat/asset)\n- Bond recognition (functional love, not romantic)\n- Prediction of institutional response\n- Turning 1 trajectory noted\n- Setup for N02 external pressure\n- Auditor's interest: useful anomaly\n\n**Purpose:** Provide meta-context, establish stakes for next novella, show Auditor's perspective on protagonists as pieces in larger game\n\n---\n\n## INTERLUDE PLACEMENT SUMMARY\n\n- **Interlude I:** After Chapter 7 (mid-Act II, after partnership forms)\n- **Interlude II:** After Chapter 10 (between crisis and resolution, shows divine forces gathering)\n- **Positioning Logic:** Interludes provide breathing space after intense chapters, offer divine/meta perspective, foreshadow external pressures coming in N02\n\n---\n\n## POV DISTRIBUTION (12 CHAPTERS)\n\n- **Jhace Solo:** 5 chapters (1, 3, 4, 6, 8)\n- **Tiffani Solo:** 4 chapters (2, 5, 7, 9)\n- **Both/Shared:** 3 chapters (10, 11, 12)\n- **Other (Interludes/Epilogue):** 3 segments\n\n**Pattern:** Opens with alternating solo POVs (establishes both independently), moves toward shared POVs as partnership deepens (reflects relationship progression)\n\n---\n\n## BEAT METHOD DISTRIBUTION\n\n**Used in N01:**\n- BEAT_01: Ch 1, 2 (acceptable consecutive for opening parallel)\n- BEAT_02: Ch 4\n- BEAT_03: Ch 3, 6, 10\n- BEAT_04: Ch 5\n- BEAT_12: Ch 8\n- BEAT_16: Ch 9\n- BEAT_17: Ch 11\n- BEAT_20: Ch 7\n- BEAT_21: Ch 12\n\n**Total Unique Beats:** 9 different methods across 12 chapters\n**Repeated Beats:** BEAT_03 used 3x (acceptable—crisis beat for crisis-heavy novella)\n\n---\n\n## EMOTIONAL INTENSITY MAP\n\n**Chapter Intensity Distribution:**\n- **High (4 chapters):** 3, 10, 11, 12\n- **Medium (6 chapters):** 4, 5, 6, 8, 9, 12\n- **Low (2 chapters):** 1, 2, 7\n\n**Arc Pattern:** Low opening → High crisis → Medium stabilization → High final crisis → Medium resolution\n\n---\n\n## SOULPULSE USAGE TRACKING\n\n**Total Uses in N01:** 7 confirmed uses\n- Ch 3: 2 uses (beam, building support)\n- Ch 4: 1 use (triage wall) + 1 failed attempt\n- Ch 6: 1 use (arch coordination)\n- Ch 8: 1 use (efficiency test)\n- Ch 10: 1 use (main support, near-Stage 2)\n\n**Stage Progression:**\n- Ch 1-2: Stage 0 (pre-Piercing)\n- Ch 3: Stage 0 → 1 onset\n- Ch 4-9: Stage 1 baseline\n- Ch 10: Approaching Stage 2 threshold (warning signs)\n- Ch 11-12: Stage 1 stabilized with management\n\n---\n\n## RELATIONSHIP PROGRESSION TRACKING\n\n**Arc Across N01:**\n- Ch 1-2: External awareness (reputation only)\n- Ch 3: First meeting (competence recognition)\n- Ch 4-5: Deliberate observation (measurement phase)\n- Ch 6: First coordination (partnership action)\n- Ch 7-8: Trust building (resistance → acceptance)\n- Ch 9: First explicit choice (each other over safety)\n- Ch 10: Conflict through care (control vs. autonomy)\n- Ch 11: Trauma bonding (vulnerability exchange)\n- Ch 12: Commitment formalized (shared principles)\n\n**By N01 End:** Load-bearing partnership established, functional love (not romantic confession)\n\n---\n\n## WORD COUNT BUDGET\n\n| Segment | Target | Status |\n|---------|--------|--------|\n| Ch 1 | 1,240 | 🔒 Locked |\n| Ch 2 | 1,280 | 🔒 Locked |\n| Ch 3 | 2,200 | 🔒 Locked |\n| Ch 4 | 2,350 | 🔒 Locked |\n| Ch 5 | 2,100 | 🔒 Locked |\n| Ch 6 | ~1,800 | To Write |\n| Ch 7 | ~1,500 | To Write |\n| I-1 | ~700 | To Write |\n| Ch 8 | ~1,600 | To Write |\n| Ch 9 | ~1,700 | To Write |\n| Ch 10 | ~1,800 | To Write |\n| I-2 | ~800 | To Write |\n| Ch 11 | ~1,900 | To Write |\n| Ch 12 | ~1,600 | To Write |\n| Epi | ~900 | To Write |\n| **TOTAL** | **~20,470** | **45% Complete** |\n\n---\n\n## FORBIDDEN SEQUENCES (N01 CREATES)\n\n**Two-Chapter Sequences That Cannot Repeat in Future Novellas:**\n1. BEAT_01 → BEAT_01 (Ch 1→2)\n2. BEAT_01 → BEAT_03 (Ch 2→3)\n3. BEAT_03 → BEAT_02 (Ch 3→4)\n4. BEAT_02 → BEAT_04 (Ch 4→5)\n5. BEAT_04 → BEAT_03 (Ch 5→6)\n\n**Three-Chapter Sequences:**\n1. BEAT_01 → BEAT_01 → BEAT_03 (Ch 1→2→3)\n2. BEAT_03 → BEAT_02 → BEAT_04 (Ch 3→4→5)\n\n---\n\n## CONSTRAINT REMINDERS\n\n**All chapters must maintain:**\n- ✅ Lexicon compliance (Tide/Kindling/Striking/Pouring/Quench/Anneal, no Earth terms)\n- ✅ Voice consistency (Jhace terse/physical, Tiffani analytical/precise)\n- ✅ No metaphors/similes (direct description only)\n- ✅ Minimal personification (structural language acceptable)\n- ✅ Cost visibility (Stage progression shown through physical markers)\n- ✅ Knowledge gates (no future reveals, no divine mechanics explained)\n- ✅ Innocent frame (characters don't know they're god-vessels)\n\n---\n\n## GRID STATUS\n\n**Version:** 2.0 (12-Chapter Structure)\n**Date Created:** [Current]\n**Supersedes:** Previous 15-chapter grid\n**Authority:** Mandatory reference for all N01 prose generation\n**Next Review:** After Ch 6 completion (validate structure holds)\n\n---\n\n**END OF GRID**\n\n**Remember: This grid is law. Any deviation requires explicit authorization and grid update before proceeding.**"

    "fb45cf27-5cf7-4048-86c5-e6e11fe6ab05": {

        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "# Chapter 4: After the Shudder  \n\nJhace woke to stone under his shoulders and a smell of boiled herbs.\n\nFor a breath he thought he was still in the street. Then the sounds sorted: not collapse, but low talk. Cloth rustling. Metal in basins. Somewhere a child crying, then hushed. No roar in the walls, only a roughened grid thrum under everything.\n\nHis hands hurt.\n\nHe tried to flex his fingers. Tremor ran from fingertips to elbow in a fine, constant shake. Pain followed, a dull burn along bone. Someone had wrapped rough cloth around his palms; the bandage edges scraped.\n\n“Stay down,” a voice said. Bram.\n\nJhace turned his head. He lay on a low pallet in a side room off a wider tunnel. The ceiling sat higher than an Underface run, stone mortared in regular blocks. Someone had cleared space and laid out more pallets in two rows. A triage nook, then. He recognized the top of a ladder shaft in the next room, the one near Bram’s corridor.\n\nBram sat on an overturned crate a pace away, elbows on his knees, face drawn. A bruise had started along one cheekbone, dark under dust. He held a clay cup in both hands.\n\n“How long,” Jhace said. His voice came out rough.\n\n“Since late Striking,” Bram answered. “We’re in Tide One now. Kindling’s moving. You were out for most of a phase.”  \n\nJhace pushed himself up onto an elbow. The room spun once and steadied. His stomach rolled; he ignored it.\n\n“Chloen,” he said.\n\n“Alive.” Bram’s mouth twitched. “Leg is… odd. She should have lost it. Healer says crushed bone, torn flesh. But when they cleaned it, some of the worst breaks were already knitting. Still bad. Not that bad. She’s under enough chew-root to sleep through a collapse.”\n\nJhace’s gaze slid past him. Chloen lay three pallets down. Her leg was splinted from hip to ankle, wrapped in clean cloth, stained through in a few places. Her chest rose and fell slow and even. Copper shimmer clung faintly at the edges of the bandages.\n\nJhace’s fingers jerked. Memory came in pieces: timber weight, heat in his hands, copper-gold sliding into bruised flesh for a flash.\n\nHe swallowed.\n\n“Do not do that,” Bram said quietly. “Do not put that on your list. She’s breathing because of you. Whatever else that was.”\n\nJhace looked down at his own hands. The cloth around them was discolored with dust and a faint copper stain. Under the bandages, his fingers twitched in small, useless patterns.\n\n“What do they say happened,” he asked.\n\n“Out there?” Bram jerked his chin toward the doorway. “Depends who you ask. Some say a falling building caught itself. Some say the Piercing tore the rules and Saint Merro’s stones are waking up angry. Some say there’s a Forgemaster with molten hands walking the streets.” His eyes went back to Jhace’s bandages. “Plenty say a grid rat in the Underface held a whole corner on his own.”\n\nJhace’s stomach tightened.\n\n“They’re asking for you,” Bram went on. “The ones who were under that wall. Runners keep sticking their heads in here. Asking if the man who held it is ready to go again.”\n\n“I held a span,” Jhace said. His throat tightened around the word. “It did not fall because the loads settled on another path. They want the wrong thing.”\n\n“Doesn’t change what they want,” Bram said. He set the cup on the floor. “City’s cracked. Council is arguing in the Crown. The guilds are yelling at each other about whose task this is. Meanwhile the stone fails where it pleases. We have work.”\n\nJhace swung his legs over the side of the pallet. His feet hit cold stone. Tremor raced up his arms in answer to the shift.\n\nBram reached to steady him. Jhace shook his head once. He pushed himself upright without help. The room lurched, then steadied.\n\n“Bad idea,” Bram muttered. “Very on-brand.”\n\n“Show me what failed,” Jhace said.\n\nBram held his gaze for a few breaths, then sighed.\n\n“Fine. But I walk beside you. If you fall, you don’t take a brace with you.”\n\nJhace said nothing. He bent to pull the bandages loose enough for his fingers to move. The cloth stuck in a few places; pain flared. The tremor did not care.\n\nThey stepped out into the main tunnel.\n\nThis level had become a passage more crowded than it had ever been. People moved in steady lines: runners with messages, guild workers with tools, healers with baskets. A smell of blood and hot metal hung under the usual damp stone. The grid’s hum had shifted; it ran harsher now, edges rough where pipes had taken damage.\n\nAs Jhace and Bram merged into the flow, heads turned.\n\n“That’s him,” someone murmured. “The one from the street.”\n\nJhace kept his eyes on the tunnel ahead. He let their words slide off.\n\n“Where first,” he asked.\n\n“Nearest failures we know about.” Bram pointed river-side. “Two marry points deformed. One small support crack we can’t spare full crews for yet. Figured we’d start there. Test the grid, test your legs.”\n\n“Test my hands,” Jhace said.\n\nBram’s mouth flattened.\n\nThey reached the first damaged junction after a short walk. The brace there had sheared partially away from the wall. Temporary wedges and a strap had been set to carry most of the load until a full crew could replace it. The main itself hummed steady; the bracket, not so much.\n\nJhace set his fingertips against the stone.\n\nThe vibration there told a clear story: stress transferred, not perfect but stable enough for now.\n\n“We move on,” he said.\n\nThe next problem sat deeper, in a lower run where the air grew hotter. A side support there had cracked along its base. Someone had braced it with a stack of loose stone, but the contact was poor; gaps showed.\n\n“No one under this line?” Jhace asked.\n\n“Not if they listened,” Bram said. “Street above already cleared.”\n\nJhace studied the crack. Not immediate collapse. Not harmless either. A failure here in the next Tide would drop a section of tunnel and weaken the wall of the building over it.\n\nIt would be days before the guild could spare enough workers.\n\nHe put his palm on the broken base.\n\nStone met skin. Cool under the surface, warmer where it held load. He could feel the fracture: a thin gap, rough edges ready to spread. Tremor in his hand muddied the read.\n\nHe closed his eyes.\n\nHold, he told whatever this new thing was. Hold here. Small span. No one is under it, but they will be. That should have been enough.\n\nNothing changed.\n\nNo heat surge. No copper-gold. Only the familiar push of load against his hand and the tremor in his own fingers.\n\nHe pressed harder. Pain flared in his palm, then his wrist. The crack did not answer.\n\nBram watched from a pace away.\n\n“Anything?” he asked.\n\n“No,” Jhace said.\n\n“Good,” Bram said. “Because if you were about to tell me you can now fix every hairline scratch in this city with your glow, I was going to go lie down in traffic.”\n\nJhace pulled his hand back. His skin tingled from the effort, but the stone stayed unchanged.\n\n“Not on demand,” he said.\n\n“So we learn something,” Bram said. “Whatever that was, it’s not a new wrench you can hang on your belt. Needs more than a bad seam.”\n\nMore than a bad seam. He thought of Chloen’s leg under the beam. The faces under the building corner. His own head and chest had been full of one thing then: do not let this kill them.\n\nHe flexed his fingers. Tremor answered, unchanged.\n\n“Mark this for priority reinforcement,” he said. “We do it by hand.”\n\nThey climbed back toward the surface.\n\nThe next failure found them before they reached open air.\n\nA runner nearly collided with them at a junction. A boy, sweat-slick, chest heaving.\n\n“You Jhace Torrins?” the boy gasped. “The holder?”\n\nJhace’s shoulders tightened.\n\n“Yes,” Bram said, before Jhace could decide whether to argue with the word. “What’s failing?”\n\n“Small shop front near the Merro kiln corridor,” the boy said. “Wall cracked. Healers set up inside, working triage. Master said to get the holder, or we lose half the wounded when it goes.”\n\nJhace didn’t look at Bram. He just started moving.\n\nThe street near the kilns was a mess.\n\nOne whole row of smaller buildings had taken impact from falling debris. Most still stood, but faces were fractured, bits of stone missing, wooden flanges splintered. Someone had chosen a shop with only one outer wall to convert into a healer’s space. The wounded lay on tables and pallets inside. The wall that separated them from the street now showed a visible crack from lintel to ground.\n\nPeople crowded the doorway. Some tried to brace the wall with poles and stacked debris. The fracture deepened a grain at a time as the crowd jostled.\n\n“Back,” Jhace said.\n\nNo one moved at first. His voice came out raw, not loud.\n\nBram stepped up beside him.\n\n“You heard him,” Bram said. “Back. Give stone room to breathe.”\n\nThe crowd parted in jerks and mutters. Jhace stepped to the crack.\n\nUp close, it looked worse. Fine dust leaked from it in a slow stream. The load path for the roof had shifted onto two narrow points instead of a full line. The shop’s inner ceiling beams rested on that.\n\nSeveral wounded lay directly under them.\n\nJhace put his palms flat on the wall at the crack’s middle. Cold from outside, heat from bodies inside, pressure from above.\n\nHold, he thought. They die if you do not hold.\n\nHeat rose under his hands. Not as sharp as under the beam or the street support, but fast enough. Copper-gold bloomed across his skin and slid into the stone, thin lines chasing the fracture up and down. They reached the lintel and the floor, then flowed sideways into the adjoining blocks.\n\nHe gritted his teeth. His arms went rigid.\n\n“Clear under the center span,” Bram shouted. “Move the pallets outward. Go!”\n\nVoices answered in fragments. He heard scrambling inside. Cloth dragged. Someone groaned in pain as they were shifted.\n\nThe wall pressed against his hands, trying to move. The crack wanted to widen. He could feel every point that carried too much now. It was more information than he had ever wanted. His pulse beat inside his fingertips.\n\nHis own shake intensified. The copper-gold in his forearms lit vein-lines down to his elbows. Every heartbeat drove a fresh spike of pain along them.\n\n“Almost,” Bram called. “Hold.”\n\nJhace held.\n\nBreaths blurred. The glow spread as far as it could find stress. Then it stopped. It did not mend. It only kept the worst from moving.\n\n“Clear,” Bram shouted at last. “Everyone out from center.”\n\nJhace counted three heartbeats more, then let go.\n\nThe copper-gold cut off. The wall shifted a small amount, then caught on a new angle, the crack now filled with fine stone dust and glass-bright residue. Some loose chips fell and bounced off the cleared floor. The ceiling beams groaned but stayed where they were.\n\nJhace’s hands dropped to his sides. His fingers clawed without his consent, opening and closing in jittering motions.\n\n“Out,” Bram told the crowd. “No one rests under that span. You—” he pointed at a pair of workers “—get poles and a proper brace on it before next Pouring.”\n\nJhace stepped back until his shoulders hit the opposite wall. The world had gone slightly out of focus at the edges. Sweat dripped down his back. His hands shook hard enough that his nails clicked against his own skin.\n\n“Your arms,” Bram said, low. “Veins went bright all the way up. Then nothing. You still with me?”\n\n“For now,” Jhace said. His tongue felt thick.\n\nHe looked down. Faint copper-gold lines still traced his forearms, pulsing in time with the tremor. They faded slowly, but the shake did not.\n\n“Well,” Bram said. “We learned the other half.”\n\n“What half,” Jhace asked.\n\n“Whatever this is,” Bram said, “it answers when people are about to get crushed. It does not answer when you want to fix a crack for later. And every time it listens, it takes more out of you.”\n\nJhace didn’t answer. He stared at his hands until the copper traces vanished, leaving only red skin and the unsteady twitch.\n\nSomeone cleared their throat to his left.\n\nThe woman from the street stood there. The one with glass dust in her hair and the logbook clutched to her chest. She had cleaned up some since he’d seen her last; the dust now lined her clothes instead of her face. Her eyes were sharper than before, if that was possible.\n\n“Jhace Torrins,” she said.\n\nHe narrowed his eyes.\n\n“You know my name.”\n\n“You shouted it at the wall yesterday,” she said. “Bram yelled it three times. And half the runners are using it now.” She looked at his hands. “May I see where you touched?”\n\n“No,” he said.\n\nHer brows lifted a fraction.\n\n“I need to understand what changed in the stone,” she said. “People are setting up triage in buildings you’ve… supported. If I know where it’s strongest, I can tell them where not to lie down.”\n\nJhace exhaled through his teeth. That, at least, was an argument he could not throw aside.\n\nHe turned back to the cracked wall and set one shaking finger against the point where the glow had felt thickest. The motion was clumsy. His fingertip missed twice before finding the line.\n\n“Here,” he said. “And here.” He traced the path down and across as best he could.\n\nShe stepped in closer, careful not to touch him. Her gaze followed his hand, then left it, measuring the stone on its own.\n\n“The temperature is wrong,” she murmured. “Hot along that line, cooler elsewhere. The stress reads different too.” She pressed her palm to the wall, eyes half closed. “It shouldn’t be standing. But it is.”\n\n“It is standing for now,” Jhace said. “Do not trust it for more than that.”\n\n“I don’t trust anything for more than that,” she said. “But I prefer numbers to stories. Stories are already taking root about you.”\n\n“Stories are useless,” Jhace said. “Either the brace holds or it doesn’t.”\n\n“And if it only holds because you touch it?” she asked.\n\nHe had no answer.\n\nShe looked down at his hands again. The tremor had not eased. He tried to still his fingers; they shook anyway.\n\n“Is that new?” she asked.\n\n“Old injury,” he said. The lie came fast, worn from too much practice over the Tide. “Tools do that.”\n\n“Mm,” she said. She did not sound convinced. “If you keep lying badly, you’re going to make my work harder.”\n\nBefore he could reply, someone called her name from up the street. She glanced toward the sound, then back at him.\n\n“I’m Tiffani Merrow,” she said. “Glasswork and structural optics.” She nodded toward the wall. “I intend to make sure people don’t sleep where your grips will fail first. That requires data.”\n\n“I have routes,” Jhace said. “I don’t have data.”\n\n“You have both,” she said. “You just don’t write all of it down yet.”\n\nShe stepped away, already scanning the next stack of damaged fronts, already turning the scene into columns in her head. Bram watched her go with a low whistle.\n\n“She’s going to be trouble,” he said.\n\nJhace opened his mouth to tell him he already had enough trouble and dropped the words.\n\nA bent nail lay near his boot. He bent to pick it up.\n\nHis fingers missed it twice. On the third try he managed to trap it between thumb and forefinger, but the tremor jerked it free. It clinked back to stone.\n\nHe stared at it.\n\nBram’s gaze slid toward his hands.\n\nJhace straightened slowly. He put his hands behind his back, hiding the twitch.\n\n“I’m fine,” he said.\n\nBram did not answer.\n\nAcross the street, Tiffani watched them. Her eyes dropped once to Jhace’s hidden hands, then back to his face. She said nothing either.\n\nThe grid hummed at its new pitch under their feet. The wall held. Jhace’s grip did not."
          ]
        },
